The report was closed with prayer by TE Welden, and the report of the Seminary was postponed until later. [See also Appendix F, p. 468.]

“Divide Westminster Presbytery into Two Presbyteries” Whereas, Westminster has grown to include 26 churches; and Whereas, there are significant doctrinal differences among the brethren within Westminster Presbytery, and Whereas^ these doctrinal differences produce significant difference s in philosophy and practice of ministry; and Whereas, we have sought for years to reconcile our differences, but have come to the conclusion—that—the—differences—betw een—as—afe—tee—fundamental— for reconciliation; and Whereas, these differences have so divided us that we are no longer able to work together effectively as a court of the church, and Whereas, such present theological division prevents us from promoting the kingdom of God in all respects, including church planting, evangelism, Christian education; u llli Whereas, at the present -time , we are able to separate ourselves in a respectful manner without bitterness or rancor, and Whereas, there is a lack of regional cohesiveness in the presbytery duet o its spanning two states and historical precedence for a geographical division along these lines; and Whereas, present boundaries do not promote shared ministries due to distance and travel time required, and Whereas, these differences in our Presbytery generally exist between churches that are separated along geographical boundaries, and Whereas, for the common good of the churches within westminster Presbytery; Now therefore, be it resolved, that Westminster Presbytery hereby overtures the 28th General Assembly of the Presbyterian Church in America to make a geographical division o

“Dissolve Northeast Presbytery; Form Three New Presbyteries” For the 28th General Assembly of the Presbyterian Church in America to form three new presbyteries out of the existing northeast presbytery (thereby dissolving the existing Northeast Presbytery) as follows: 1. Southern New England Presbytery— Geographical Boundaries: Connecticut (excluding the CT "panhandle" that is currently in Metro Presbytery), Rhode Island and Massachusetts. 2. New York State Presbytery— Geographical Boundaries: The boundaries include all the Churches, ministers and territories in New York State, excluding those churches, ministers and territories under the jurisdiction of metropolitan new york presbytery and ascension Presbytery ; and further, subject to the approval of ascension Presbytery, to include the churches, ministers and territories within New York State but are currently under the jurisdiction of ascension Presbytery. 3. Northen New England Presbytery— Geographical Boundaries: Maine, New Hampshire, and Vermont Rationale: 1. Geographical- Whereas, the formation of the above constituted presbyteries would significantly ease the burden of vast geographical distances between churches and would have access to the major travel corridors that move through the region; and Whereas, the above stated boundaries are reflective of natural travel corridors as follows: Southern New England—A Southern New England Presbytery would significantly ease the burden of vast geographical distances between churches and would have access to the major travel corridors that move through the tri-state region (1

“Reject Covenant College’s Response to Protest” Whereas, our high calling extends beyond the local congregation to the regional and national levels of the church (Deuteronomy 13:12-18), and Whereas, part of this responsibility involves the maintaining of truth and righteousness in all of the agencies of the General Assembly; and Whereas, the Covenant College Board of Trustees has acknowledged that a choir from Covenant College sang during the observance of the Roman Catholic mass; and Whereas, the Board has also responded to a protest which was lodged during the 27th General Assembly with regard to this matter; and Whereas, in making response to this protest, the Board has tried to make a distinction between singing in a mass and participating in it; and Whereas, this is a distinction without a difference; and Whereas, no competent church historian or theologian would recognize such a distinction; and Whereas, the Roman Catholic Church itself does not regard this distinction as valid; and Whereas, our Confessional Standards are clear, that the doctrine of transubstantiation “is repungnant, not to Scripture alone, but even to common sense, and reason; and overthroweth the nature of the sacrament, and hath been, and is, the cause of manifold superstitions; yea, of gross idolatries” (Confession of Faith, XXIX, 5); and Whereas, the doctrine of transubstantiation is integral ly tied with the celebration of the mass, and Whereas, our Confessional Standards also declare the truth that “the popish sacrifice of the mass is most abominably injurious to Christ’s one, only sacrifice, t

From the Presbytery of Mississippi Valley “Authorize Formation of a Provisional Presbytery in Russia” **Whereas**, TE Blake Purcell is a member of Mississippi Valley Presbytery, and **Whereas**, Mississippi Valley Presbytery has authorized TE Blake Purcell to serve out of bounds as an evangelist in St. Petersburg, Russia, and **Whereas**, Mississippi Valley Presbytery has entrusted to TE Blake Purcell (BCO 8-6) "the power to organize churches, and, until there is a Session in the church so organized, to instruct, examine, ordain, and install ruling elders and deacons therein, and to receive or dismiss members;" **Therefore, Be it Resolved** that Mississippi Valley Presbytery overture the 28th General Assembly of the Presbyterian Church in America, according to BCO 15-6, to exercise its "power to commit to a commission, consisting of not less than three elders, the task of forming a provisional Presbytery in a foreign country [Russia] where there exists no compatible indigenous presbyterian and reformed church. Such a commission shall have authority to act as the Presbytery in all matters pertaining to the establishment and ordering of a national church and shall report annually to the General Assembly. The commission shall be dissolved when there are at least three national teaching elders and three organized churches under its care, and these shall then constitute a separate national church." Adopted at the Winter Stated Meeting of The Presbytery of the Mississippi Valley on February 15, 2000. Attested by: /s/ Roger G. Collins, Stated Clerk 10. That **Personal Resolution #

source: OVERTURE 14 From New Jersey Presbytery (to AC)
body: “Erect committee to develop funding of General Assembly” Whereas, the General Assembly consists of all teaching elders (TE) who are in good standing in their Presbyteries and Ruling Elders (RE) elected by their Sessions (BCO 14-2), and Whereas, congregations are entitled to two RE representatives for the first 350 communing members or fraction thereof, and one additional RE for each additional 500 communing members or fraction thereof, (BCO 14-2), and Whereas, at the 27th General Assembly the ratio oft e stores was over 2 to 1 (854 to 374), and it has long been the concern oft hep ca that there be more RE representation from the congregations, and Whereas, small congregations often lack the financial ability to send one representative let alone two or three, and Whereas, representatives of small congregations often use creative ways to save money and still get to General Assembly meeting, (i.e. sharing rides, using buddy fares on planes, sharing rooms with representatives from other congregations, staying in inexpensive second-class motels, etc.), and Whereas, small congregations may be able to find creative ways to send more than one representative to General Assembly without large increases in travel and lodging costs, (i.e. sharing rides, using buddy fares on planes, sharing rooms, having representatives pay their own travel and meal costs, etc.), and Whereas, the increases in the registration fee for attending the General Assembly is making it more and more difficult for small congregations to send representatives, while large congregations are less impacted by the incr

“Condemn the SJC Decision in the John Wood Case” Whereas, Presbyterianism historically has allowed for one General Assembly to condemn the opinion of another General Assembly (M 12GA, p. 140: "A subsequent General Assembly may take a contrary position and condemn the action of a previous Assembly."); and Whereas, the decisions of the Standing Judicial Commission (SJC) are those of the previous General Assembly (BCO 15-4: "and each subsequent Assembly should declare the Standing Judicial Commission as a whole to be is commission”), and Whereas, the General Assembly is bound "to bear testimony against error in doctrine ., injuriously affecting the Church.” (BCO 14-6.a ); and Whereas, the decision oft hes jc in re Presbyterian Church in America vs. John Wood is evidently flawed, and Whereas, because of the peculiar nature of current p ca judicial process, the General Assembly as such never was able directly to review this decision of the SJC; and Whereas, the decision is flawed in numerous ways, especially in its interpretation of the Constitution; and Whereas, among the errors in Constitutional interpretation are the following: (1) t hes jc assumes that a corporate responsibility by a session means that individual members cannot also be held responsible for the action of the session; (2) the SJC assumes that there were no doctrinal issues involved, but merely the action of inviting a woman to fill the pulpit, when there is evidence that Mr. Wood advocates female preaching; (3) the SJC assumes that action by Tennessee Valley Presbytery to vindicate the session of cedar springs

2. BCO 34-1 has two prerequisites which must be met before the matter oft e john Woods/Cedar Springs PCA can properly be before the GA. The SJC has determined that Tennessee Valley Presbytery has indeed acted. Therefore, since the first prerequisite has not been met, the GA has no jurisdiction in this matter. 3. The Resolutions from the three Presbyteries were directed by the Presbyteries to the GA and were considered by the GA via the SJC. 4. The GA has established the SJC as the Assembly’s normal process for handling cases of this kind (BCO 15-4, “The General Assembly shall erect a Standing Judicial Commission to which it shall commit all judicial cases within its jurisdiction"). 5. The GA via the SJC found these resolutions to be out of order because the grounds upon which they sought the GA to act via the SJC were wrong, namely the Tennessee Valley Presbytery had acted on the matter of TE John Wood and his Session. 6. The SJC also noted that while some members of the Tennessee Valley Presbytery protested the Presbytery’s investigation, no members of the Presbytery sought to bring that protest to the SJC via a complaint. 7. As the SJC has acted on behalf of the GA it would be quite inappropriate for the GA and the SJC to take this case up a second time. Further, it would be quite inappropriate for the GA, in effect, to investigate a TE a second time when he has been cleared of wrong doing by his Presbytery. Such a second investigation would amount to an act of double jeopardy. 8. In addition, the BCO and the SJC Manual provide for only the most limited circumstances for

source: OVERTURE 9 From the Presbytery of the Ascension
body: “Amend BCO 24-1” Whereas, the 26th GA adopted a proposed amendment to BCO 24-1 (printed below); and Whereas, the grounds cited by Central Carolina Presbytery (which brought the original overture) and by the Bills & Overtures Committee were that the requirements of BCO 24-1 requiring (1) that there be at least one month’s prior notice of a meeting for electing officers and (2) that Session inform the congregation before election day of those eligible for election “can place undue time constraints on the Session.” (M26GA, pp. 208-209); and Whereas, that amendment is still pending because there were not sufficient presbyteries voting in favor of it (M27GA, pp. 58-60); and Whereas, at least 13 presbyteries have voted against this proposed amendment (M27GA, pp. 58-60); and Whereas, the reasons for the negative votes seem to be tied to (1) the absence in the new language of a clear statement that it is the Session which must examine the candidates and (2) confusion regarding the meaning of the second paragraph of the amendment [i.e., does the “30 days” requirement mean that the names of eligible nominees must be reported to the congregation at the time of the call for the meeting (which the order of the events in 24-1 seems to imply) or does it simply speak to the required notice for the meeting while leaving the Session the discretion as to when to report the names of eligible nominees (and thus allowing the Session to not report those names prior to the meeting if it so desires)?]; and Whereas, it seems to us that it is reasonable to require 30 days notice of a meeting for

“Grant Full Constitutional Status to the Entire Directory of Worship” Whereas, it has always been the practice of Presbyterian denominations around the world to include some form of a directory for the public worship of God within their constitutional standards; and Whereas, the public worship of God is a principal, and the most visible, act performed by the Church of the Lord Jesus Christ; and Whereas, the Bible enjoins all Christians to worship in a fitting and orderly way (1 Corinthians 14:40); and Whereas, the matter of public worship is one of considerable debate and increasing tension within the Presbyterian Church in America; and Whereas, the current Directory for Worship of the Presbyterian Church in America is only “an approved guide” and “does not have the force of law and is not to be considered obligatory” in most of its parts; Therefore, be it resolved that Grace Presbytery overture the General Assembly of the Presbyterian Church in America to rescind the current prefatory statement to the Directory For Worship, and in so doing grant the Directory fo r Worship full constitutional status and weight. Adopted this 11th day of May, in the year of our Lord, nineteen hundred, ninety- nine, by the presbytery of grace i nits ninety-fourth stated meeting, at Hazlehurst, Mississippi. Attested by: /s/ E. C. Case, Stated Clerk 5. That OVERTURE 12 from Potomac Presbytery (“Prohibit Women from Preaching in Public Worship”) be answered in the affirmative as amended Add a new clause at BCO 12.5d., after the words “Bible study groups;”, as follows: to encourage the women in the

“Prohibit Women from Preaching in Public Worship” Whereas, questions have been raised across the church concerning the legitimate role of women in the PCA, and whether the constitution of the PCA is sufficiently explicit to guard against unbiblical practices with respect to the ministry of women in our churches; Therefore, Potomac Presbytery respectfully overtures the 28th General Assembly of the Presbyterian Church in America to proceed to the amendment of Book of Church Order to the following effect: add a new clause at BCO 12.5d., after the words “Bible study groups;”, as follows: to encourage the women in the church to hold meetings and give instruction appropriate to their calling (e.g. Titus 2:3-5), while prohibiting women from expounding or preaching the word of god a san ordinance of public worship (cf. BCO 4-4; 53-2), as this is clearly forbidden in Scripture (1 Tim. 2:11-12); Adopted by Potomac Presbytery at the 41st Stated Meeting, February 12, 2000. Attested by: /s/ Richard R. Larson, Stated Clerk S. That OVERTURE 10 from the presbytery of mississippi valley (“Restrict Preaching or Exhorting to Qualified Men Only”) be answered in the negative (See also Majority Rationale, p. 80 and Minority Reports, p 87, 89) Adopted Grounds: The overture asks the Assembly to replace the language in question If the request is taken literally, it can only be acceded to if the views argued in the minority report of the cc bare correct, and the DFW in this section is not a part of the Constitution. But in that case, there is little point in the adjustment, since the affected docume

source: OVERTURE 10 from the presbytery of mississippi valley
body: “Restrict Preaching or Exhorting to Qualified Men Only” Whereas, the Westminster Confession of Faith, Chapter XXI, paragraph 5 discusses the elements of corporate worship, and “sound preaching. . .” is an element of corporate worship, and Whereas, god ordained men to bethe spiritual leaders of israel (Ex. 18: 17-26), and Whereas, the priesthood of israel was composed only of the sons of aaron (Ex. 28: 1-4), and Whereas, the apostles of Jesus, used of god to establish the new covenant Church for our Lord, were all men (Mt. 10: 1-4), and Whereas, the Bible forbids women to teach or exercise authority over men (I Tim. 2: 11-15), and Whereas, Larger Catechism, question 158 states: “.. .the word of g odis to be preached only by such as are sufficiently gifted and also duly approved and called to that office,” and Whereas, God forbids ordained office to women (I Tim. 3: 2,12, Titus 1: 6), and Whereas, the entire bible teaches the principle of covenant heads hip of men over women (I Cor. 11: 3), a principle which is usurped when women exhort or instruct men in the church, Let it Therefore Be Resolved that Mississippi Valley Presbytery overture the 28th General Assembly of the Presbyterian Church in America to replace Book of Church Order paragraph 53-6, which currently reads: “No persons hall be invited to preach in any of the churches under our care without the consent of the Session, ” with the following: “Only qualified men with the consent of the sessions hall be invited to preach/exhort in any of the churches under our care. ” adopted at t hew interstate d meeting of the pres

“Revise BCO 56-5 Regarding ‘Our Holy Religion.’” Whereas, the first paragraph of *The Directory for the Worship of God*, states that “BCO 56, 57 and 58 have been given full constitutional authority by the Eleventh General Assembly after being submitted to the Presbyteries and receiving the necessary two-thirds (2/3) approval of the Presbyteries,” and Whereas, BCO 56-5 states, regarding the questions a minister is to ask when doing an infant baptism, “The minister shall then propose the following questions…”, and Whereas, the word “shall” requires the minister to use the specific wording of the questions as recorded in BCO 56-5, and Whereas, in question three the term “our holy religion” is used, and Whereas, in modern English the term “religion” carries with it the idea of a man-made belief system in that there are numerous world religions, and Whereas, Colossians 3:23 specifically uses the term “religion” in a negative sense referring to man-made religion, and Whereas, the BCO 56-5 use of the term “our” also adds to the connection to Colossians 3:23 about religions as being of human origin, and Whereas, the intent of BCO 56-5 is that of encouraging the teaching of the true doctrines given to the Church by the Lord Jesus as revealed in the Bible, Therefore, be it so ordered that the BCO be revised by the following action: Delete the phrase “our holy religion” and add the phrase “the holy Bible”, in BCO 56-5, so that question three will read, in part, “…that you will teach (him) the doctrines of the holy Bible, and…” Not adopted by the Presbytery of New Jersey. [RAO 10-10] A

“Revise BCO 56-5 Regarding “Means of God’s Appointment” Whereas, the first paragraph of the directory for the worship of God, states that “BCO 56, 57 and 58 have been given full constitutional authority by the Eleventh General Assembly after being submitted to the Presbyteries and receiving the necessary two-thirds (2/3) approval of the Presbyteries,” and Whereas, BCO 56-5 states, regarding the questions a minister is to ask when doing an infant baptism, “The minister shall then propose the following questions. . .”, and Whereas, the word “shall” requires the minister to use the specific wording of the questions as recorded in BCO 56-5, and Whereas, in BCO 56-5, question three contains the archaic phrase “o f God’s appointment”, Therefore, be it so ordered that the BCO be revised by the following action: Delete the phrase “o f God’s appointment” and add the phrase “appointed by God” in BCO 56-5, so that question three will read, in part, . and that you will strive, by all the means appointed by God, to bring (him) up in the nurture and admonition of the Lord?” not adopted by the presbytery of new Jersey. [RAO 10-10] Attested by: /s/ James A. Smith, Stated Clerk 8. That OVERTURE 17 from Philadelphia Presbytery (“Add Vow for Elders when Receiving Members”) be answered in the negative. Adopted Grounds: such avow is already affirmed by the elders of the church (cf. BCO 21-5.7. -5 .8 ; 24-5.4).

“Add Vow for Elders when Receiving Members” Whereas, elders are called upon to shepherd the flocks that the Chief Shepherd has placed under their care (1 Peter 5:1-5), and Whereas, new church members come under the shepherding care and authority of the elders of the church that they join, and Whereas, new church members are called upon to assent their submission to “the government and discipline of the church” represented by the elder- shepherds of the church (BCO 57-5, Question 5), and Whereas, church membership represents not merely the commitment of the member to the authority of the church, butt he commitment of the church to the care of new members; Therefore, we propose that the elders of the church express their commitment to shepherd their new members by adding this Question Six under BCO 57-5: 6. To the elders of the church (standing): Do you, as elders and shepherds of this congregation, commit yourselves to provide spiritual care, nurture, and discipline to these who have submitted themselves to your God-given authority, recognizing that they are now among the sheep for whom you will give an account to the Chief Shepherd? Adopted November 13, 1999, by the presbytery of philadelphia for presentation to the 28th General Assembly, 2000, in Tampa, Florida Attested by: /s/ Frank D Moser, Stated Clerk 9. That OVERTURE 18 from Ellisville PCA, Ellisville, MS (“Amend BCO 13-1 to Allow Additional Ruling Elders as Presbytery Commissioners”) be answered in the negative Adopted Grounds: the problem of limited ruling elder participation doe snot appear to be a function of the

source: OVERTURE 18 From Ellisville PCA, Ellisville, MS
body: “Amend BCO 13-1 to Allow Additional Ruling Elders as Presbytery Commissioners” Whereas, the formation of the Presbyterian Church in America was the result of the Lord, in His Providence, convicting elders of the need to remain true to the Biblical principles and teachings upon which our faith is based, and Whereas, our elder fathers in the faith were the first to point out and take a stand against errors being made and taught in the old Southern church, and Whereas, ruling elders have played an integral part in the organization and growth of the Presbyterian Church in America, serving diligently in all courts of the church, and Whereas, the parity of the elders hi pis compromised when the composition of church courts contain substantially more teaching elder commissioners than ruling elder commissioners, and Whereas, ruling elders serve the church generally, when called thereunto (BCO 8-3; Acts 20:28f), as well as the local congregation, and Whereas, the General Assembly of the Presbyterian Church in America recognized the need for more ruling elder involvement and participation in said court by amending the Book of Church Order to increase the number of ruling elder commissioners from each church from one ( 1) to two (2), with additional commissioners for larger congregations, and Whereas, there is a similar need for more ruling elder involvement and participation in the church presbyteries. Now Therefore, Be it Resolved, that the undersigned Session overtures the General Assembly of the Presbyterian Church in America to amend the Book of Church Order, 13-1, to increase th

“Amend BCO 16-4 to Allow that Exceptions May Not Be Taught” the session of the cherokee presbyterian church respectfully requests that the North Georgia Presbytery nevertheless forward for consideration by the PCA ’s General Assembly in June, 2000, the following overture (to be accompanied by notice of the Presbytery’s negative vote on the overture at its fall meeting): We hereby request the 2000 General Assembly oft hep ca proceed with the process to add to the BCO, Chapter 16 on Church Orders the following Paragraph 16-4: “When the process of examination reveals any exceptions to the constitutional standards that the relevant court judges, if taught, to be harmful to the peace and well-being of the church (though not serious enough to disqualify the candidate for office), it may by a majority vote direct the candidate for office not to teach the exception.” Rationale: this clarifies by making explicit to all what is understood by many to bean implicit power of the courts of the church. thank you for your consideration of this request. Letter dated January 7, 2000 and signed by Rev. Ted Lester, Moderator, for the session of cherokee presbyterian church of Woodstock, Georgia. NOTE: North Georgia Presbytery had previously rendered a negative vote upon its earlier consideration. at the last meeting of the presbytery of north Georgia, on January 15, 2000, the Committee on Bills and Overtures in their report, instructed the clerk to forward this overture to the office of the stated Clerk, Presbyterian Church in America, for handling. 11. That OVERTURE 13 from Susquehanna Valley

“Re-ordination of former p ca Ministers” whereas the Book of Church Order rightly calls fora presbytery to divest a minister if he continues on the rolls without call for over three years (BCO 34-10, 13-2), and Whereas, this procedure is rarely followed, and Whereas, this leads to men not being as closely connected to sessions as is good forth em ora sis good for the work of the church, and Whereas, this often puts pressure on presbyteries to approve “calls” which are questionable, and Whereas, one reason for this failure of presbyteries to act is that both the man and his presbytery are hoping a call will come and if it does after he is divested he would have to go through the entire ordination process again, Therefore, the Presbytery of Susquehanna Valley hereby overtures the General Assembly to add a new section 5 as follows to chapter 21 of the BCO and renumber the following sections: NEW 21-5: Modified procedures may be used for the re-ordination of a former PCA minister who receives a call to a particular church after he was divested without censure at his request (38-2) or was divested of his office without censure against his will (34-10). The presbytery in which he is being called shall carefully investigate the circumstances under which he was divested. If the presbytery decides that the reason he was divested was simply a lack of call, it may proceed to give him the trials for ordination outlined in 21-4 without going through the normal candidate, licensure, and internship procedures. At its discretion, the presbytery may accept his previous ordination in lieu of

“Clarify use of professional counsel in cases of Process” Whereas, BCO 32-19 forbids the use of professional counsel “as such” in cases of process but permits an accused to be represented before the session of the church of the accused by a member of that church, and before any other court by a member of that court; and Whereas, the phrase “as such” is subject to different interpretations; and Whereas, the limitation on representation before a court higher than the Session may destroy the continuity of representation of the accused and otherwise prejudice his defense, Therefore, Be it Resolved that the James River Presbytery respectfully overtures the General Assembly of the Presbyterian Church in America to clarify, amend, and restate BCO 32-19 so that it will read in its entirety as follows: 32-19 An accused person may be counseled and represented before the Session or any other court by any communing member of the same particular church of which the accused is a member, orb y any member o for commissioner to a court before which the accused appears, provided, however, that the individual serving as counsel may not serve for a fee or other remuneration Notwithstanding the foregoing, payments may be made to reimburse or cover any direct expenses incurred or to be incurred by such counsel in the course of representation. If such counsel is a member o for commissioner to the court before which the accused appears, he may not sit in judgment in the case. adopted at the spring stated meeting of james river presbyter yon april 8, 2000. Attested by: /s/ Eugene H. Friedline, Stat

source: OVERTURE 7 from presbytery of north florida
body: “Decry contentious practices of the ‘Presbyterian and Reformed News’” Whereas, the Presbyterian and Reformed News, in an apparent disregard for the wisdom of Proverbs 6:16-19, [There are six things which the Lord hates, Yes, seven which are an abomination to Him: haughty eyes, a lying tongue, and hands that shed innocent blood, a heart that devises wicked plans, feet that run rapidly to evil, a false witness who utters lies, and one who spreads strife among brothers, (NASB)] has printed stories and information that has created much confusion and fomented division within the PCA, and Whereas, the Presbyterian and Reformed News has printed statements even though the person or persons have asked not to be quoted (Spring 1998 issue, p. 5), and Whereas, the Presbyterian and Reformed News has printed statements about a member of North Florida Presbytery that called into question his reputation, integrity and Christian character (New Bulletin #27-1, June 14-15, 1999), and Whereas, the Presbyterian and Reformed News has printed summaries of the minutes of North Florida Presbytery, Therefore Be it Resolved, that the Presbytery of North Florida overtures the Presbytery of the Northeast to instruct the Editor(s) of the Presbyterian and Reformed News that Christian charity demands that they more seriously consider and humbly respect the requests of Christian brothers and Presbyteries. Ephesians 4:3 [I, therefore, the prisoner of the Lord, entreat you to walk in a manner worthy of the calling with which you have been called, with all humility and gentleness, with patience, showing forbe

“Improper Dissemination of Information Regarding Judicial Cases and Information Detrimental to Reputations” Whereas, the Lord, through the Apostle Paul, urges believers to live “with all humility and gentleness, with patience, showing forbearance to one another in love, being diligent to preserve the unity of the Spirit in the bond of peace” (Ephesians 4:2-3 – NASB); and Whereas, the authors of the Westminster Larger Catechism explain that the Ninth Commandment requires of us “the preserving and promoting of truth between man and man, and the good name of our neighbor, as well as our own;…a charitable esteem of our neighbors; loving, desiring, and rejoicing in their good name; sorrowing for, and covering of their infirmities;…a ready receiving of a good report, and unwillingness to admit of an evil report, concerning them;” (WLC 144); and Whereas, the Book of Church Order (PCA) in 27-1 says, “Discipline is the exercise of authority given the Church by the Lord Jesus to instruct and guide its members and to promote it’s purity and welfare”; and Whereas, the Book of Church Order (PCA) 27-5 says, “Scripture law is the basis of all discipline because it is the revelation of God’s Holy will. Proper disciplinary principles are set forth in the Scriptures and must be followed. They are: - **a.** Instruction in the Word; - **b.** Individual’s responsibility to admonish one another; - **c.** If the admonition is rejected, then the calling of one or more witnesses; - **d.** If rejection persists, then the Church must act through her court unto admonition, suspension, excommunication

“Amend BCO 38-4 by Deletion” Whereas, BCO 38-4 creates a method for administrative removal of a member from the church of the lord jesus christ without formal judicial process under certain specified circumstances. Whereas, the first sentence of the second paragraph of b co 38-4 reads thusly: “If after diligently pursuing such pastoral discipline, and after further inquiry and due delay, the session is of the judgment that the member will not fulfill his membership obligations in this or any other branch of the visible church (cf. BCO 2-2), then the Session shall erase his name from the roll.” Whereas, the highlighted words create serious constitutional problems for a Session faithfully attempting to exercise its duties under BCO 38-4, inasmuch as it might be reasonably inferred that administrative removal from the membership roll may NOT occur under this provision if the member were regularly worshipping and participating in another church other than the one in which he took his membership vows. Whereas, the highlighted words may be reasonably interpreted to require a Session to exercise formal discipline rather than administrative removal, if a member ignored his membership vows and began attending another congregation. Whereas, it is the intent of scripture an dour constitutional standards that decisions pertaining to the election of officers and well-being of particular congregations should be decided by those members who are faithful to their membership vows. Whereas, the Session has the constitutional authority to receive, remove and dismiss members. BCO 12-5(a). Wher

“Amend RAO 14-7-3 by Adding Another Sentence” WHEREAS, the purpose of the committee on review of presbytery records of the General Assembly is stated in RAO 14, and WHEREAS, the change in RAO 14-7-3 by last year’s General Assembly moves the committee on review of presbytery records away fr omits stated purpose by making it determine the theology and practice of the PCA, THEREFORE BE IT RESOLVED that RAO 14-7-3 be amended tore adas follows: 1. minutes approved with exception of substance which shall be presented to the General Assembly, which presentation include citation of any relevant Scriptural, Constitutional and /or RAO references and provide the Committee’s rationale for finding the exception of substance. The citation(s) and rationale are only to be seen as advice bv the General Assembly through her committee to help the Stated Clerk and the Presbytery cited to frame a satisfactory response to the citation. [Changes to current RAO underlined ] Reason: the committee on review of the presbytery records should not be in the business of framing doctrine and practice for the PCA. The committee’s work is to approve minutes and if exceptions of substance are found, to bring to General Assembly to vote on them. All references should b ea help to a stated clerk and a presbytery to frame a satisfactory response to the next General Assembly. It should be up to the Assembly on motion from the floor to deal with the proper recourse of an egregious exception. An example would be a man coming for ordination denied the Virgin Birth and was still approved for ordination by a Presbyte

Lunceford, Stated Clerk whereas full pr et eris mi sade ni aloft he doctrine of the future, visible, physical, second coming of our lord and savior jesus Christ, and whereas full pr et eris mi sade ni aloft he biblical doctrine that the just and the unjust await a resurrection of their present mortal bodies from the dust, and whereas full pr et eris mi sade ni aloft he biblical doctrine o faye t future final judgment, and whereas these three doctrines are fundamentals of the reformed system of doctrine contained in the Westminster Confession of Faith and the Larger and Shorter Catechisms, and Whereas the Apostles’ Creed, the Nicene Creed, and the Athanasian creed all declare these doctrines to be fundamentals of the Christian Faith, and Whereas many serious doctrinal deviations can and have logically flowed from these denials, and whereas the implications of these doctrinal deviations affect the whole system

“Support North Korean Refugees” The Korean Southeastern Presbytery overtures the 28th General Assembly to: 1. Write an official letter to the Chairman of the United Nations requesting that the U.N. provide protection for the refugees escaping from North Korea and that refugee camps be established to provide shelter for them. 2. Write an official letter to the President of The United States requesting that the United States provide protection for the refugees escaping from North Korea and that refugee camps be established to provide shelter for them. 3. Set aside a special time of prayer during this General Assembly for the refugees who are escaping from North Korea. 4. Give permission to post a sign-up sheet for all commissioners who would like to continue supporting these refugees in prayer. 5. Give permission to send a letter to each local church requesting them to support these refugees by special prayer. Adopted by the Korean Southeastern Presbytery at its 29th Stated Meeting on April 3, 2000. Attested by: /s/ Dan Dongkyo Lee, Stated Clerk

body: “Request Assembly to Assume Original Jurisdiction” Whereas, duet o the public nature of the letter from teaching elder john wood and the session of cedar springs presbyterian church dated october 25, 1999 to the Tennessee Valley Presbytery, it is our concern that this overture be handled in a timely and God-honoring manner. Whereas, the Scriptures clearly teach that a woman is not to teach or exercise authority over a man, but is to remain silent (1 Timothy 2:12); and Whereas, t hep ca Book of Church Order should be interpreted consistently rather than in a way that is contrary to the Scriptures; and Whereas, to permit a woman to preach or teach in the context of worship would b ea gross violation of 1 Timothy 2:12; and Whereas, the session of cedar springs presbyterian church has made it known that a woman has been invited to be the plenary speaker for the 2001 World Missions Conference, Therefore Be it Resolved, that James River Presbytery overtures the General Assembly as Western Carolina, Calvary and Ascension Presbyteries have done in objecting to the Cedar Springs Presbyterian Church’s use of female Preachers in their Worship service, and request the 28th General Assembly of the Presbyterian Church in America to assume original jurisdiction in this matter in accordance with the provisions of b co 14-6 and BCO 34-1. adopted at the spring stated meeting of james river presbyter yon april 11, 2000. Attested by: /s/ Eugene H. Friedline, Stated Clerk Commissioners Present: Presbytery Commissioner Ascension RE Frederick (Jay) Neikirk, Secretary Central Carolina RE Wilson Ba

source: Susquehanna Valley Presbytery: Amend BCO 20-2 to Include Pastor in Search Committee for Assistant or Associate Pastor
body: It is the opinion of the CCB that Overture #3 is in conflict with the other parts of the Constitution. Teaching Elders are not members of the local congregation and therefore cannot be a member of the congregation’s pulpit committee. The proposed mandate of the overture that they be so would interfere with the right of the congregation to determine those who will rule over them, a privilege which undergirds BCO 20. Having said that, the Committee does recognize the wisdom of consulting with the senior pastor in the selection of an Assistant or Associate Pastor. *Adopted by CCB*

source: Eastern Carolina Presbytery: Change Procedure for Pastoral Relationship Dissolution
body: It is the opinion of the CCB that Overture #4 is not in conflict with the other parts of the Constitution. It is also the opinion of the CCB that the offered revision offers very little substantive change to our present standards when they are properly understood and carried out – except for the proposed provision that a Commission can never be used by a presbytery to dissolve the pastoral relationship which seems unnecessarily restrictive for perfunctory cases without objection from any party. *Adopted by CCB*

source: Calvary Presbytery: Require Exceptions for Views Other Than Six 24-Hour Creation Days
body: It is the opinion of the CCB that Overtures 7, 20, 23, and 30 touch on issues that are not properly before the CCB since they do not seek constitutional amendment or constitutional advice. a. The Creation Study Committee Report has the status of any other study committee report, such as those on the gifts of the Holy Spirit and Freemasonry, that is, it is the thinking of a particular General Assembly on a specific subject. b. The Creation Study Committee Report, as with all other study committee reports adopted by a General Assembly, does not amend the constitution (BCO Preface, III. “The Constitution Defined” and BCO 26-1); only amendments effected by the proper constitutional procedures are actual and lawful amendments (BCO 26-2, 26-3 and 26-6). c. The Creation Study Committee Report has the status of being among those decisions of the General Assembly that are to be given “due and serious consideration by the Church and its lower courts when deliberating matters related to such actions” (BCO 14-7). d. Overtures relating to the Creation Study Committee Report would have the same status as the report, i.e. of being among those decisions of the General Assembly that are to be given “due and serious consideration by the Church and its lower courts when deliberating matters related to such actions” (BCO 14-7). e. It is the purview of the lower courts to determine the suitability of men applying for reception/ordination with regard to the men’s views on the matters raised in *in thesi* statements, subject always to proper review and/or judicial process. (BCO 13-6 and 13-9.a) (

source: Presbytery of the Ascension to ask CCB to Examine the Minutes of the SJC
body: In response to the Overture from the Presbytery of the Ascension, the CCB understands the SJC to have acted in accord with GA’s directive to proceed according to the provisions of BCO 31-2 which required the SJC to enter an “investigation” phase of the matter before determining if there was sufficient evidence that “should result in raising a strong presumption of the guilt of the party involved” before instituting process and proceeding to trial. Presbyteries were informed by letter (dated August 29, 2000) of : (1) their right to submit evidence supporting their allegations to the SJC panel during this investigative phase; and (2) the time and place to submit evidentiary material related to the investigation. The understanding of the SJC was that further theological argument was appropriate for a trial, should an indictment have been rendered. Since no indictment was made, however, on the basis of the evidence presented by all notified parties it would have been inappropriate to try John Wood on the basis of theological arguments against matters neither he, nor the SJC, felt he supported. Had the matter moved to trial, it would have been proper for the presbyteries to make arguments for their allegations of theological error. The CCB acknowledges that, according to “Whereas #7-9” in Ascension Presbytery’s Overture, it is unfortunate that there was no formal, written response from the SJC to Ascension Presbytery’s inquiry following the panel’s initial contact and prior to its hearing. In order for presbyteries to feel that they have been adequately heard and fairly treated,

“Divide Heartland Presbytery” Whereas, the geographical boundaries of heartland presbytery are among the largest in the PCA; and, Whereas, the churches within the boundaries of iowan ow number 6 organized churches numbering 813 members; two mission; and one possible church transferring from Independency; and, Whereas, stewardship of the local churches relates to time, talent and tithes; and, with the current distances, it is difficult to m aintain close fellowship due to the travel time required; and, Whereas, requests from other Presbyteries with fewer churches and members have been approved (recognizing the same difficulties as Heartland); THEREFORE BE IT RESOLVED, that Heartland Presbytery be divided so that the State of Iowa would become a separate Presbytery of the PCA effective January 1, 2002. Adopted at the 36th Stated Meeting of Heartland Presbytery, on November 11, 2000. Attested by: /s/ Lawrence N. Lunceford, Stated Clerk 10. source: OVERTURE 12 From Central Carolina Presbytery (to MNA)
body: “Divide Central Carolina Presbytery into Two Presbyteries” Whereas, the Central Carolina Presbytery exists for the Glory of God and the furtherance on His Kingdom here on Earth; Whereas, the Central Carolina Presbytery currently consists of Forty-Two (42) particular and mission congregations comprising more than Six Thousand (6,000) communing members within a geographical area requiring substantially more than three hours to cross; Whereas, the 16th General Assembly adopted guidelines for the multiplication of existing presbyteries into new presbyteries, which guidelines provide a set of ideals governing the development of new presbyteries; Whereas, pursuant to those guidelines, the Central Carolina Presbytery committed itself to a plan to grow into three separate presbyteries several years ago, which plan included developing three sub-regions (Metrolina, Triad, and Sandhills) within the presbytery and focusing church planting resources on developing new churches within each of these regions; Whereas, the Triad Region of the Central Carolina Presbytery has grown to Six (6) particular congregations and Two (2) mission congregations comprising more than One Thousand communing members and these churches have developed a strong sense of mutual care and potential for shared ministry and joint ministry projects; Whereas, Five of the particular congregations located in the Triad Region have communing membership in excess of One Hundred Twenty-Five, with strong prospects for continued growth in the future; Whereas, there is a commonly recognized regional identity within the Eleven

source: OVERTURE 22 from Great Lakes Presbytery (to MNA)
body: "Divide Great Lakes Presbytery and Form Ohio Valley Presbytery" Now Therefore, Be it Resolved, that Great Lakes Presbytery unanimously overtures the 29th General Assembly to form a new “Ohio Valley” Presbytery by: Establishing the boundary of the continuing Great Lakes Presbytery to include: All the state of Michigan; All of Indiana north and east of and including the counties of Porter, Jasper, Pulaski, Fulton, Wabash, Huntington, Wells, and Adams; and All of Ohio currently in Great Lakes Presbytery north and east of and including the counties of Mercer, Shelby, Logan, Union, Madison, Pickaway, Hocking, Vinton, and Meigs. Establishing the boundary of the new Ohio Valley Presbytery to include: All of Indiana that is currently in the Great Lakes Presbytery and is south and west of but not including the counties of Porter, Jasper, Pulaski, Fulton, Wabash, Huntington, Wells, and Adams; and All of Ohio south and west of but not including the counties of Mercer, Shelby, Logan, Union, Madison, Pickaway, Hocking, Vinton and Meigs. All of the counties of Kentucky currently in Great Lakes Presbytery. This action is to be effective July 1, 2001. Adopted unanimously at the Stated Meeting of the Great Lakes Presbytery held on October 13, 2000 12. That the General Assembly approve the 2002 budget of Reformed University Ministries, and note the budget including the affiliated campus ministries is provided to the General Assembly for its information; and further, that the Assembly note with thanksgiving the opportunities and challenges represented by the entire 2002 budget. source: OVERTURE 26 From New York State Presbytery (to MNA)
body: “Redraw New York State and Ascension Presbytery Boundaries” Whereas, the current western boundary of the New York State Presbytery includes central New York east and including Wayne, Ontario and Steuben counties; and, Whereas, the current eastern boundary of the Ascension Presbytery includes western New York west and including Monroe, Livingston and Allegany counties; and Whereas, the city of Rochester located in Monroe County and within the boundary of Ascension Presbytery has no PCA church; and Whereas, there are several families who have expressed interest in the past several years in forming a core group for the purpose of starting a PCA church in the Rochester area; and Whereas, there are some families who travel from the Rochester area to New Life Presbyterian Church in Ithaca, NY, a mission church in the New York State Presbytery, for worship; and Whereas, these same families have indicated a desire to affiliate with the New York State Presbytery; and Whereas, the New York State Presbytery would like to initiate and oversee this developing PCA work in the Rochester area as part of its vision to plant healthy and reproducing PCA churches in every major city in upstate New York; Therefore, Be It Resolved that the New York State Presbytery hereby overtures the 29th General Assembly of the Presbyterian Church in America to redraw the western boundary of New York State Presbytery and the northeastern New York boundary of Ascension Presbytery, to wit, that the counties east of and including Monroe and Livingston be transferred to New York State Presbytery, and furthermore,

source: OVERTURE 29 From TE Andrew Lee of Northern California Presbytery
body: “Direct MNA to Report Administrative Fees” Whereas, in October 1997 the GA MNA Committee approved a policy: (a) to limit the maximum dollar amount (or percentage), of "Field Assistance and Project Development Costs" [project overhead/administrative fees] taken from funds raised for church planting projects; and (b) to disclose to select donors the existence and amounts of such costs as a percentage of donations; and Whereas, prior to October 1997, i.e., without aforementioned Committee-approved policy, GA MNA Staff prepared and administered church planting project budgets that included "Field Assistance and Project Development Costs" [project overhead/administrative fees]; and Whereas, GA MNA heretofore has not been a member of the Evangelical Council for Financial Accountability (ECFA; www.ecfa.org) as has another PCA Committee (Mission to the World) and PCA Agency (Covenant Theological Seminary) - organizations that are also involved in fundraising (Romans 12: 17b). (It should be noted that while GA MNA's financial records are audited annually by an independent firm, and all records are reviewed by normal General Assembly processes, ECFA membership is helpful both in terms of accountability and in increasing the confidence of donors); and Whereas, GA MNA as a Committee of the PCA is accountable to the General Assembly; Therefore, Be It Resolved that the General Assembly have GA MNA report to the General Assembly on the following: 1. Written policy and limits that GA MNA Staff used to determine "Field Assistance and Project Development Costs" [and/or similar overhead/admin

"J & R of Mid-America Presbytery into North Texas Presbytery" Whereas, Mid-America Presbytery, at times in its history having boundaries encompassing all of Oklahoma and parts of Kansas, Missouri, and Arkansas, and as many as ten churches, now, by the departure of churches and territory on several occasions to other expanding presbyteries, has been reduced to only part of the state of Oklahoma and to only four remaining churches with a combined communing membership of between four and five hundred, and Whereas, Mid-America Presbytery has the fewest churches of any of the contiguous PCA presbyteries, and Whereas, Mid-America Presbytery does not believe it currently has the resources of churches, presbyters, funds and morale most effectively to staff appropriately presbytery committees and commissions and move toward further growth, and Whereas, Mid-America Presbytery desires to gain encouragement towards future growth by being a part of a presbytery experiencing growth by church planting and the establishment of campus ministries, and Whereas, Oklahoma City and a corridor of counties connecting it to Texas are already part of North Texas Presbytery where currently, with the help of the Southwest Church Planting Network, two new Oklahoma City area church plants and a Reformed University Fellowship at the University of Oklahoma are being established, and Whereas, being received into North Texas Presbytery would immediately provide our churches with the option of participating in the three-presbytery, Southwest Church Planting Network (North Texas, South Texas and Southwest

*(to MNA)* “Revise Boundaries of Westminster Presbytery” Whereas, the counties of Pulaski and Carroll lie geographically in the New River Valley and at the extreme northeastern end of Westminster Presbytery, closer to all the churches in New River than to those in the entire southern cluster in Westminster; and Whereas, in the formative studies of potential boundaries for Westminster and New River Presbyteries, significant consideration was given to the possibility of our two Counties being assigned to New River; and Whereas, the geographical and cultural issues involving our two churches and those in New River Presbytery have led to very positive ministry and outreach sharing, particularly between the Virginia congregations of that Presbytery and ourselves; and Whereas, New River Presbytery has indicated a desire to receive those two Counties with a notation that adding our two Counties would strengthen their presbytery without unduly weakening a stronger Westminster; and Whereas, New River Presbytery has formed an ad-hoc committee to explore with some of the churches in James River Presbytery, and our two churches, the possibility of asking the General Assembly in the future to form a new Presbytery in the west-central area of Virginia; Now Therefore, be it resolved that Westminster Presbytery herewith overtures the 29th General Assembly of the Presbyterian Church in America to redraw the boundaries of Westminster and New River Presbyteries by transferring Pulaski County and that portion of Carroll County that lies east of Interstate 77 in the Commonwealth of Virginia, an

source: OVERTURE 36 From Central Carolina Presbytery (to MNA)
body: “North Hills PCA in Salisbury, NC, Seeking Admission into Newly-formed Triad Presbytery” Whereas, North Hills Presbyterian Church exists for the Glory of God and the furtherance of His Kingdom on Earth; Whereas, North Hills Presbyterian Church was admitted to the Central Carolina Presbytery in October of 1998 and has been blessed by its association with Central Carolina Presbytery and the Presbyterian Church in America; Whereas, North Hills Presbyterian Church is located in Rowan County, a county that adjoins the proposed boundaries of the Triad Presbytery; Whereas, North Hills Presbyterian Church lies in the extreme north of Rowan County next to the proposed boundary of the Triad Presbytery; Whereas, North Hills Presbyterian Church has a strong affinity to the Triad geographical region as well as to the churches in the new presbytery; Whereas, this proximity and affinity has already resulted in filial and ministry ties to the churches in the new presbytery; Whereas, official connection with the Triad Presbytery would strengthen North Hills Presbyterian Church, further its mission, and encourage its members to love and good deeds; Now Therefore North Hills Presbyterian Church hereby petitions and overtures the Central Carolina Presbytery and the General Assembly that it be allowed to join the newly-formed Triad Presbytery outside its geographical bounds effective July 1, 2001. Adopted by the Moderator of Session, North Hills Presbyterian Church and the Clerk of Session, North Hills Presbyterian Church /s/ Jerry C. Currin, Moderator and /s/ M. J. Smith, III, Clerk of Session

“Divide Potomac Presbytery and Form Chesapeake Presbytery.” Now Therefore, Be it Resolved, that Potomac Presbytery overtures the 29th General Assembly to form – effective January 1, 2002 – a new “Chesapeake” Presbytery by: 1. establishing the boundary of the continuing potomac presbytery to include: • the city of w ashington, D.C. • the maryland counties of Calvert, Charles, Frederick, Montgomery, Prince George’s, Saint Mary’s, and Washington. • the west virginia counties of Berkeley, Jefferson, and Morgan. • the virginia counties of Alexandria, Arlington, Clarke, Fairfax, Fauquier, Frederick, Prince William, and Loudoun, including the cities within their borders. 2. establishing the boundary of the new chesapeake presbytery to include: • the city of baltimore and the maryland counties of Baltimore, Carroll, Harford, Howard, Anne Arundel; and Kent Island in Queen A nne’s County. approved at the called meeting of potomac pres byte ryh eldon march 31, 2001. adopted at the stated meeting of potomac pres byte ryh eldon may 15, 2001. “Erect New Presbytery” westminster presbytery hereby notifies the General Assembly o fits intention to withdraw from the Presbyterian Church in America, effective at the January 2002 stated meeting of Presbytery; and the General Assembly be overtured to take the following action: That the General Assembly, under the powers granted to it under the PCA Constitution to erect new presbyteries and to unite and divide presbyteries per their request, hereby established a new presbytery, in accordance with the following criteria: those churches and ministers which have certified to the stated clerk of Westminster Presbytery by the January 2002 stated meeting of said presbytery that they wish to remain in Westminster Presbytery, shall be constituted as Westminster Presbytery; while those churches and ministers which have not certified to the stated clerk of westminster presbytery by the january 2002 stated meeting of said presbytery that they wish to remain in westminster Presbytery, shall be constituted as a new Presbytery, effective that date, with the same boundaries as the present Westminster Presbytery. Adopted by Westminster Presbytery on May 15, 2001. Attested by: Is/ D. Steven Meyerhoff, Stated Clerk 25. that communication 5 from the Westminster Presbytery be received by the 29th General Assembly as supporting information to overture 38, and that, in order to honor the original conditions under which Westminster Presbytery joined the PCA, the communication be spread upon the minutes of the 29th General Assembly. the committees response to Recommendation 24 above answers Comm

source: OVERTURE 1 from Missouri Presbytery (to CCB & B&O)
body: “Amend BCO 14-1(12), Increase Number on Committees, Extend Terms to Five Years” Whereas, the founders of our denomination, in order to minimize the concentration of power in the hierarchy of our denomination, created the structure of our General Assembly Committees and Agencies so that Committee members would be elected, in classes, for 4-year terms and are only eligible for re-election after a year has elapsed, Whereas, the responsibility and complexity of our Program Committees have grown as our denomination has grown, Whereas, it is very difficult for newly elected Committee members to function effectively until their 4-year term is almost half over, Whereas, this frequent turnover of Committee members coupled with the increased size and complexity of Committee operations tends to place more power in the hands of the permanent staff which is opposed to the intent of the founders of our denomination, Whereas, the proposed change provides for greater continuity in our Program Committees while still providing for the regular rotation of Committee members, Therefore Be it Resolved that BCO paragraph 14-1(12) in the second to the last paragraph be revised as follows: “Committee on Christian Education and Publications, Committee on Mission to North America, Committee on Mission to the World, and Committee on Reformed University Ministries shall consist of fifteen (15) men divided into five classes of three men each, with two men being TEs and one RE or two men being REs and one TE on alternate years on odd years and four men each on even years, elected to serve five-year terms

source: OVERTURE 2 from New Jersey Presbytery (to CCB & B&O)
body: “Amend BCO 58-5 – Use Unleavened Bread in Communion” Whereas, the first paragraph of *The Directory for the Worship of God*, states that “BCO 56, 57 and 58 have been given full constitutional authority by the Eleventh General Assembly after being submitted to the Presbyteries and receiving the necessary two-thirds (2/3) approval of the Presbyteries,” and Whereas, Exodus 12:8, 15-20 reveals that the Passover celebration and accompanying Feast of Unleavened Bread used unleavened bread, Whereas, Luke 22:1 reveals that the Lord Jesus, when He instituted the Lord’s Supper, was sharing a Passover meal with His disciples, and Whereas, 1 Corinthians 5:7 specifically states that believers are “unleavened” because Christ “our Passover” has been sacrificed, and Whereas, “leaven” is sometimes used in the Bible to denote sin, (cf. Matthew 16:6, 11-12), and Whereas, Jesus Christ is sinless, (Hebrews 4:15), Therefore, be it so ordered that the BCO 58-5 be revised by the insertion of the word “unleavened” before each occurrence of the word “bread” in the first, second, and fourth paragraphs and by adding these words at the end of the first paragraph: “If unleavened bread is unavailable, bread with leaven (yeast) may be used, but this is not to be the usual or normal practice of a congregation.” The first four paragraphs of BCO 58-5 shall now read (added words are underlined): “The table, on which the elements are placed, being decently covered, and furnished with *unleavened* bread and wine, and the communicants orderly and gravely sitting around it (or in their seats before it), the elder

source: OVERTURE 3 from Susquehanna Valley Presbytery (to CCB & B&O)
body: “Amend BCO 20-2 to Include Pastor in Search Committee for Assistant or Associate Pastor” Whereas, the *Book of Church Order* does not address the matter of searching for an assistant or associate pastor, and Whereas, for the peace and unity of the church it is vital that there be harmony among the pastoral staff of a church, and Whereas, any new assistant or associate pastor must be agreeable to and compatible with the senior pastor, Therefore be it resolved that the following be added to the BCO 20-2: “When a search committee for an associate pastor is formed by a congregation, the senior pastor shall automatically be a member of the committee. Before proposing a candidate to the congregation, the committee shall assure itself that the candidate is acceptable to and has the full support of the senior pastor. “In the case of a call to an assistant pastor, Session may act as the search committee or it may appoint church members as a search committee to bring a candidate for Session approval. In either case, the senior pastor shall be a member of the search committee.” Adopted by the Susquehanna Valley Presbytery on September 16, 2000. Attested by: D. F. Rundle, Stated Clerk 4. That Overture 4 be answered in the negative. Adopted as amended

source: OVERTURE 4 from Eastern Carolina Presbytery (to CCB & B&O)
body: “Change Procedure for Pastoral Relationship Dissolution, BCO 23-1” Be it resolved that the Book of Church Order (BCO) 23-1 be amended as follows: **Existing provision**: BCO 23-1, first two paragraphs “BCO 23-1. When any minister shall tender the resignation of his pastoral charge to Presbytery, the Presbytery shall cite the church to appear by its commissioners, to show cause why the Presbytery should or should not accept the resignation. If the church fail to appear, or if its reasons for retaining its pastor be deemed insufficient, his resignation shall be accepted and the pastoral relation dissolved. If any church desires to be relieved of its pastor, a similar procedure shall be observed. But whether the minister or the church initiates proceedings for a dissolution of the relation, there shall always be a meeting of the congregation called and conducted in the same manner as the call of the pastor. In any case, the minister must not physically leave the field until the Presbytery or its commission empowered to handle uncontested requests for dissolution has dissolved the relation.” The **proposed revision** of the first two paragraphs only, follows: “BCO 23-1. When any minister shall tender the resignation of his pastoral charge to Presbytery, the Presbytery shall cite the church to appear by its commissioners, to show cause why the Presbytery should or should not accept the resignation. A meeting of the congregation shall be called and conducted in the same manner as the call of a pastor, and either accept the resignation or be prepared to show cause why the Presbyte

source: OVERTURE 7 From Calvary Presbytery (to CCB & B&O)
body: "Require Exceptions for Views Other Than Six 24-Hour Creation Days" Whereas, the Presbyterian Church in America is a confessional church based upon the Westminster Standards; and Whereas, our Confession of Faith states the time frame of God's original creative activity with the phrase, "in the space of six days" (WCF 4:1); and Whereas, growing historical evidence demonstrates the intent of the Westminster Assembly was to define the six creation days as 24-hour calendar days (At least 13 Westminster divines have been identified as attesting to a 24-hour creation day in their writings and recorded sermons; while none has been documented as holding to other time frames.); and Whereas, the official position of Calvary Presbytery states that the doctrine of the Confessional standards of the Presbyterian Church in America explicitly asserts that the world was created by divine fiat, literally “in the space of six days”. Because the Confession’s teaching on creation is unassailably grounded in Scripture, we joyfully proclaim that our preaching and teaching should conform to its content and emphases. (Adopted October 24, 1995); and Whereas, the 28th General Assembly affirmed that “...a diversity of views on creation days...is acceptable” thus, in essence, invalidating any definitive creedal statement; and Whereas, it has been our historic practice during elder examinations to note any differences from our standards as exceptions to be discussed by the church court; Therefore Be It Resolved, that the 29th General Assembly of the PCA declare that, with charity toward all our members,

source: OVERTURE 20 from New River Presbytery (to B&O & CCB)
body: “Allow Exceptions on Length of Creation Days” Whereas, the 28th General Assembly rejected the unanimous recommendation of the Creation Study Committee to wait two years before taking any action on the subject of the interpretation of the length of the days of creation in Genesis 1 and 2; and Whereas, the 28th General Assembly gave approval to an action which, while described by the Moderator as simply ‘pious advice’, is being perceived by many in the denomination as pressure to approve candidates for office without regard to their views on these issues, therefore, BE IT RESOLVED, that New River Presbytery, voting at its 80th Stated Meeting in Roanoke, Virginia on November 10, 2000, hereby encourages and requests the 29th General Assembly of the PCA to take the following actions as additions and further explanation of the Assembly’s response to the Creation Study Committee report: A. While the PCA recognizes both a historic and current diversity of views in the area of the length of creation days described in Genesis 1 and 2, it remains the primary and original responsibility of Presbyteries and Sessions to examine their candidates and determine if their views, no matter how diverse, are in accord with the Constitution of the church, and to determine if such views are exceptions. B. Presbyteries and Sessions who define any other view than the 24 hour day view as an exception to the standards in this area, in keeping with the actions of the 14th General Assembly, Section 143-52, Report of the Committee of Commissioners on Judicial Business, are to make the determination as to

source: OVERTURE 23 From Mississippi Valley Presbytery (to B&O &CCB) “Require Exceptions for Views Other than Six 24-Hour Creation Days”
body: Whereas, the Bible, in Genesis 1, Exodus 20:11, and Exodus 31:17 teaches that God created the heavens and the earth, the sea and all that is in them in six days, and Whereas, our doctrinal standards (WCF 4, LC 15, and SC 9) affirm that it pleased God, by the word of his power, to create the world and all things in the space of six days, and all very good. Therefore, Be It Resolved, with charity toward all members and with fidelity to the original authors of our confessional standards, our understanding is that, unless evidence to the contrary is found, the Westminster Standards’ phrase “in the space of six days” means that the six days of creation were days of normal duration with evening and morning. Accordingly, for the peace of the church, any future candidates who differ with this original meaning should request an exception to the Confession, unless documentation that the Westminster Assembly meant otherwise is firmly established. We also pledge to make these future determinations, with care, charity, honesty and impartiality. adopted at the stated meeting of mississippi valley presbyter yon february 6, 2001. Attested by: /s/R o ger G. Collins, Stated Clerk 6. that overture 30 from North Georgia Presbytery (“Affirm position of 28th assembly on diversity of views on creation Days”) be answered in the negative. Grounds: 1) it is the responsibility of the lower courts to examine and determine i fa m an’s views are in accord with the constitution, not the man himself, not the General Assembly, except in cases of judicial process. 2) Using general revelation to determine do

source: overture 30 From North Georgia Presbytery (to B&O)
body: “Affirm position of 28th assembly on diversity of views on creation Days” Be it Resolved, that the North Georgia Presbytery overtures the 29th General Assembly of the Presbyterian Church in America to affirm the position of the 28th General Assembly, allowing a diversity of creation views, and likewise, with charity toward all our members, declare liberty-of-opinion concerning the intent of the westminster divines in their use of the phrase “in the space of six days.” be it further r esolved, that the 29th General Assembly recognizes that, within the church body, there are strongly held views concerning the days of creation; and. the Assembly acknowledges that for a creedal-based church, positions of diversity-of-view and liberty-of-opinion are not permanently satisfactory. Therefore, Presbyteries, Sessions and individual church members are encouraged to explore ways by which the scientific evidence of general revelation can be objectively studied and validated so that the church can move toward a unified understanding of how god created the universe and brought the earth into existence. Meanwhile, let us rejoice in our bonds of brotherhood and the hope we share in the reformed faith until our knowledge of hi strut his more fully developed. Adopted by North Georgia Presbytery on April 17, 2001. Attested by: /s/ Dwight Allen, Stated Clerk that communication #4 from Western Carolina Presbytery be received as information, and spread upon the minutes of the 29th General Assembly. Grounds: We appreciate the concern and call for purity, peace, and unity in the denomination. A dop

"Condemn Action of SJC in Judicial Matter 99-1 (re. TE John Wood)" Whereas, the 28th General Assembly instructed the SJC to hear from the Presbyteries concerned in the charges against John Wood; and Whereas, the SJC nor its panel of three men gave the Presbyteries an opportunity to speak to the theological and constitutional issues involved in the John Wood case; and Whereas, the SJC made theological interpretations without hearing from the Presbyteries; and Whereas, the SJC was instructed to determine Mr. Wood's views, in their report they do not focus on his views but that he has not agitated for the view that women may preach in the church; and Whereas, in the report of the panel there is a conflict between the testimony of Drs. Smith and Pipa and that of Mr. Wood with respect to Mr. Wood's definition of preaching and there was no opportunity for cross examination; and Whereas, there is a conflict between the report of Mr. Wood's position in the SJC ruling and his expressed position in his letter of October 2, 1999; and Whereas, such conflict can only be resolved through a trial with cross examination; and Whereas, in its spring meeting in 2000 the SJC would not allow its members who were from the Presbyteries that brought the original overtures to the SJC to vote on the issue, even though it was pointed out that in cases of original jurisdiction no member of the court was required to recuse himself. Therefore Be It Resolved, Calvary Presbytery overtures the 29th General Assembly of the Presbyterian Church in America to condemn the report of the 28th Assembly through the

(to CCB & B&O) “Procedures on Assuming Original Jurisdiction Over Church Members” A New BCO 33-1 Process against all church members, other than ministers of the Gospel, shall be entered before the Session of the church to which such members belong, except in cases of appeal. This principle of original jurisdiction is designed to protect the right of the Session to investigate and adjudicate matters of sin within its bounds. Additionally, this principle of original jurisdiction is designed to protect the church member against judicial charges from those outside the Session who do not necessarily have access to the facts or access to the right of investigation. However, if there are other sessions in the same Presbytery which are not satisfied with how the Original Jurisdiction Session (OJS) has dealt with a matter of allegation against one of its members, then the following process shall be followed: 1. Those other sessions in the same Presbytery must formally request the OJS to conduct a BCO 31-2 investigation of allegations against or reports surrounding this member within the OJS’s jurisdiction. 2. If the OJS conducts a BCO 31-2 investigation, then the matter will remain totally within the jurisdictional bounds of the OJS, subject, of course, to BCO provisions of complaint appeal, review and control. 3. If these other sessions in the same Presbytery (which followed step one above) do not believe that a BCO 31-2 investigation has taken place within a reasonable time period, these other sessions may request their Presbytery to assume original jurisdiction. 4. If at

source: OVERTURE 13 From Central Carolina Presbytery (to CCB & B&O)
body: “Amend BCO 32-3 Regarding Sequence of Meetings of a Court in Judicial Process” Chapter 32 - General Provisions Applicable to all Cases of Process 32-3. It is appropriate that with each citation the moderator or clerk call the attention of the parties to the Rules of Discipline (BCO 27 through 46) and assist the parties to obtain access to them. When a charge is laid before the Session or Presbytery, it shall be reduced to writing and nothing shall be done at the first meeting of the court unless by consent of parties, except: 1. to appoint a prosecutor, 2. to order the indictment drawn and a copy, along with names of witnesses then known to support it, served on the accused, and 3. to cite all parties and their witnesses the accused to appear and be heard at another meeting which shall not be sooner than ten days after such citation. At the second meeting of the court, the charges shall be read to the accused, if present, and he shall be called upon to say whether he be guilty or not. If the accused confesses, the court may deal with him according to its discretion; if he plead and take issue, the trial shall proceed be scheduled and all parties and their witnesses cited to appear. The trial shall not be sooner than 14 days after such citation. Accused parties may plead in writing when they cannot be personally present. Parties necessarily absent should have counsel assigned to them. Rationale – Current wording explicitly mentions only two meetings of the court. The proposed change would result in three meetings in any full case of process – indictment meeting, arraignment,
