source: OVERTURE 15 From Central Carolina Presbytery (to CCB & B&O)
body: “Amend BCO 35-7 and 32-18 Requiring Recording of Testimony and Provision for Transcription” This overture would substitute new language for BCO 35-7, and strike one word from BCO 32-18. Chapter 35 - Evidence 35-7. Every question put to a witness shall, if required, be reduced to writing. When answered, it shall, together with the answer, be recorded, if deemed by the court or by either party of sufficient importance, and the testimony of the witness shall be read to him for his approbation and subscription. Proposed new 35-7 All testimony shall be recorded (transcription, audiotape, videotape, or some other electronic means) and witnesses informed of such prior to testifying. Such recording becomes part of the Record of the Case. However, in order to be referenced in written or oral briefs, such recording must be transcribed and the transcription authenticated by the trial court. The court of final appeal may assess the cost of transcription equitably among the parties. Chapter 32 – General Provisions Applicable to all Cases of Process 32-18 Minutes of the trial shall be kept by the clerk, which shall exhibit the charges, the answer, written record of the testimony, as defined by BCO 35-7, and all such acts, orders, and decisions of the court relating to the case, as either party may desire, and also the judgment... Rationale – The current wording of *BCO 35-7* does not ensure a complete record of all the testimony in a trial. Therefore, if an appeal or a complaint is filed, there may not be a sufficient record of the testimony. This change would ensure that a complete reco

“Ask CCB to Examine Minutes of SJC regarding Original Jurisdiction in John Wood Judicial Matter” Whereas, the 28th General Assembly voted to take jurisdiction of Dr. John Wood with respect to allegations set forth in overtures from three presbyteries; and Whereas, the matter was referred to the SJC to be considered “after the manner of BCO 31-2;” and Whereas, the 28th General Assembly directed that, as part of this process, “representatives from the petitioning presbyteries be permitted to represent their allegations before the SJC” (M28GA, p. 310); and Whereas, Presbytery received, from the Investigative Panel appointed by the SJC, a letter stating that the Panel would be meeting “...for the sole purpose of gathering information...” and asking that we identify witnesses “...who have direct knowledge of relevant facts...;” and Whereas, Presbytery understood “represent their allegations” to entail, not just a right to identify witnesses, but also a right to explain our reasoning and concerns; and Whereas, the contents of the Panel’s letter indicated to us that there would not be an opportunity for us to “represent our allegations” before the Panel in the way in which the 28th General Assembly meant that phrase; and Whereas, we sent a letter to the Panel detailing this and other concerns, and asking for a fuller explanation of the SJC’s planned process, particularly as it related to “permitting us to represent our allegations;” and Whereas, Presbytery never received a formal response to its letter; and Whereas, in a telephone call, initiated by the Stated Clerk of Presbytery,

source: OVERTURE 21 From Ellisville Presbyterian Church (to B&O & CCB)
body: “Amend BCO 13-1 to Allow at Least Two Ruling Elders to Represent Each Congregation in Presbytery Meetings” Whereas, the formation of the Presbyterian Church in America was the result of the Lord, in His Providence, convicting elders of the need to remain true to the Biblical principles and teachings upon which our faith is based, and Whereas, our elder fathers in the faith were the first to point out and take a stand against errors being made and taught in the old Southern church, and Whereas, John Edward Richard, in his History of the Presbyterian Church in America (1986, p. 131) posits, “There seems to be a desire in the pre-birth movement of the new church to give right concern and positions to all officers, whether lay or ministerial. We may even hope to return to decentralized polity so wisely advocated by James H. Thornwell near the birth of the Southern church,” and Whereas, the Scriptures of the Old Testament in 1 Kings 12:1-5, 18, which record King Rehoboam’s fateful rejecting and discarding of the wisdom of “the elders (who) had stood before his father Solomon while he still lived” and how Rehoboam instead consulted and followed only the advice of “the young men who had grown up with him, who stood before him,” clearly demonstrate the tragic results of rejecting and discarding the wisdom of old and wise elders, and Whereas, the Scriptures of the New Testament in 1 Timothy 3:1-7, especially require that the elder be a man who “is not a novice” plainly declaring the desirability and advantage of those elders who are men not only of demonstrable godliness but of rich

(to B&O) "Set Conditions for Use of Executive Session" Whereas, the Presbyterian Church in America was founded as a “grass roots” denomination; and Whereas, one of the manifestations of the “grass roots” concept is the fact that the meetings of the committees of the church were assumed to be open to all members of the church (M13GA, p. 90); and Whereas, this openness, which has been mandated by the General Assembly, is consonant with the fact that in the PCA we operate with subservient “committees” rather than semi-autonomous “boards”; and Whereas, the election of men to the various committees is an act by which the Assembly selects representatives; and Whereas, the meetings of church committees ought to be open to all the members of the denomination with the possible exception of certain highly sensitive matters, the knowledge of which being widespread might be detrimental; and Whereas, the Presbyterian Church (USA) has adopted strict guidelines for when its boards may go into executive session, so as to guarantee openness in the deliberations; and Whereas, we in the PCA should not be less open and honest than our liberal counterpart; and Whereas, this is especially the case since we self-professedly operate with committees rather than boards, as is done in the PC(USA); and Whereas, we want to preserve this openness which was set forth as the ideal in the PCA from early in its existence: Now Therefore, Be It Resolved, that Grace Presbytery hereby overtures the General Assembly of the Presbyterian Church in America to take the following actions: 1. to reaffirm that all

source: OVERTURE 25 From Grace Presbytery (to B&O & CCB) “Forbid Women to Preach or Teach in Corporate Worship”
body: Whereas, the Word of God and the Confessional Standards of the Presbyterian Church in America declare without ambiguity that a woman shall not teach or exercise authority over a man; and Whereas, this prohibition is proclaimed as a result of the creation ordinance and not for the reason of first century cultural expediency; and Whereas, no ecclesiastical body of the Presbyterian Church in America (be it congregation, session, presbytery, or assembly) may, under any assumed authority, set aside the clear teaching of God’s Word or the Westminster Standards; and Whereas, a contrary view to both Scripture and the Standards is being taught and adopted, specifically, that session may allow the instruction of men by women in ecclesiastical assembly under the “authority and oversight” of the elders; Therefore the Presbytery of Grace, meeting this 12th day of September, 2000, overtures the General Assembly of the Presbyterian Church in America: 1. to affirm that a woman may not preach or teach in the corporate worship of any church or in the corporate worship conducted by any church court or by any committee, board, and/or agency of a church court; and 2. to reaffirm that the Presbyterian Church in America believes that the Scripture teaches that the offices of Elder and Deacon are open to men only. Adopted at the 98th Stated Meeting of Grace Presbytery on the 12th day of September, 2000. Attested by: /s/ Eugene C. Case, Stated Clerk 18. That Overture 27 from the Presbytery of Philadelphia (“Amend BCO 12-5e to Prohibit Women from Preaching”) be answered in the negative. Grounds: Our

source: OVERTURE 27 From the Presbytery of Philadelphia (to CCB & B&O) "Amend BCO 12-5e to Prohibit Women from Preaching"
body: Whereas, there are some among us who believe that Scripture explicitly prohibits women from teaching and having authority over men in the church (1 Cor. 14:34, 35; 1 Tim. 2:11, 12); and Whereas, women may encourage each other and the children under them (Titus 2:3-5); and Whereas, our Confession explicitly and implicitly envisioned the ordinance of preaching and teaching, or "the ministry of the Word", to be limited to ordained male ministers of the Word (WCF 27.3, 4 [Scripture proofs]; LC 54 [Scripture proofs], 156, 158, 159, 169, cf. 176); and Whereas, the BCO teaches that candidates for the ministry of the Word (18-1), those licensed to the ministry of the Word (19-1), and interns who are to be ordained to the ministry of the Word (19-7) are to be males; and Whereas, BCO 19-1 teaches that "no man" can preach without licensure in the presbytery "where he will preach"; and Whereas, there are some who believe that there is considerable confusion within our church as to the proper and biblical role of women in regards to the ordinance of preaching and teaching during stated worship services; and Whereas, there are some who believe that Paul's prohibition was primarily related to the activity of teaching itself and not to the office of teaching (though it is necessarily implied, "good and necessary consequence", WCF 1:vi); and Whereas, it is impossible for women to remain silent (1 Cor. 14:34; 1 Tim. 2:12) while teaching and preaching to men during stated worship services; and Whereas, it is impossible for females to instruct men and women, to exhort from Scripture before men

source: OVERTURE 28 From the Presbytery of Philadelphia
body: “Pray for Religious Liberty” (to B&O) Whereas, the PCA is a Protestant and Reformed denomination serving our God especially in the United States of America, which celebrates its 225th anniversary of Independence this July 4th, 2001, whose original states declared themselves to be committed to religious liberty and the Protestant faith as seen in the following citations from the Journal of the First Continental Congress of 1774: THAT it is an indispensable duty which we owe to God, our country, ourselves and posterity, by all lawful ways and means in our power to maintain, defend and preserve these civil and religious rights and liberties for which many of our fathers fought, bled and died, and to hand them down entire to future generations. THAT the late act of Parliament for establishing the Roman Catholic religion and the French laws in that extensive country now called Quebec, is dangerous in an extreme degree to the Protestant religion and to the civil rights and liberties of all America; and therefore as men and protestant Christians, we are indispensably obliged to take all proper measures for our security...; And in prosecution of the same system, several late, cruel and oppressive Acts have been passed respecting the town of Boston and the Massachusetts-Bay and also an Act for extending the province of Quebec, so as to border on the western frontiers of these Colonies, establishing an arbitrary government therein, and discouraging the settlement of British subjects in that wide extended country; thus by the influence of civil principles and ancient prejudices to

“Declare Denominational Employees Not Eligible For Membership On SJC” Whereas, the Presbyterian Church in America has prided itself on being a "grass roots" denomination; and Whereas, the PCA has put this principle into effect with respect to the Standing Judicial Commission (SJC) by prohibiting anyone from serving on the SJC and a Permanent Committee of the General Assembly simultaneously (BCO 15-4); and Whereas, the same principle is seen to be at work in that "Employees of the Assembly's committees or agencies are not eligible for office on an Assembly's committee or agency which administers matters directly related to their area of employment" (RAO 4-7); and Whereas, the work of the SJC covers the denomination as a whole; and Whereas, at present, four of the twenty-four members of the SJC are denominational employees; and Whereas, it seems prudent that denominational employees should not serve simultaneously on the SJC; Now Therefore, Be It Resolved, that Mid-America Presbytery hereby overtures the 29th General Assembly to declare that no denominational employee is eligible to serve on the SJC, excepting those already elected before General Assembly’s ruling on this overture; **And Be It Further Resolved** that Mid-America Presbytery hereby overtures the 29th General Assembly to amend the Rules for Assembly Operations by adding to RAO 15-1 a sentence, immediately after the first sentence, as follows: > "No one who is employed by a denominational committee or agency may serve on the Standing Judicial Commission." Adopted at the 40th Stated Meeting of Mid-America Presb

It is evident that none come nearer to us than the Platonists.” (VIII:5) “[The reason we prefer to discuss with Platonists is because] these, have found in knowing God . . . the cause of creation . . . th

source: OVERTURE 35 From Western Carolina Presbytery (to B&O)
body: "Remove Concurring Opinion in Judicial Matter 99-1" Whereas, the Western Carolina Presbytery has issued a call to the Presbyterian Church in America to bring the John Wood matter to closure and to endeavor to pursue together both the purity, peace and unity of the church; and Whereas, we question the propriety of the Standing Judicial Commission attaching concurring opinions to a report that they were not going to proceed to judicial process, since concurring opinions are provided for only in cases of judicial process and to include these concurring opinions tend to disturb the peace of the Church, after the decision has been reached; and Whereas, to allow the personal allegations against individual members of the Assembly to be placed in the Minutes of the General Assembly can only bring hurt to the individuals concerned, and cause resentments that are destructive of the purity, peace and unity of the Church; and Whereas, this is not the proper venue for such allegations, since TE's who have acted improperly should be charged in the courts of original jurisdiction, where they can have a fair trial; and Whereas, we do not believe that the provision for concurring opinions was ever intended to provide a forum for personal attack as occurs in this report; Therefore, Western Carolina Presbytery urges that all of the concurring opinions be removed from the SJC report, and that they not appear in the Minutes of the General Assembly. Adopted by Western Carolina Presbytery at a called meeting on May 8, 2001. Attested by: /s/ Skip Gillikin, Stated Clerk 23. That Personal Resolution

“Ask AC to Provide Options for Lower Costs and Greater RE Participation at General Assembly” Whereas, the PCA has from its inception subscribed to the parity of Elders, and, Whereas, since the first GA in 1973 when the enrollment was 208 Ruling Elders and 179 Teaching Elders, the disparity, between Ruling Elders and Teaching Elders has grown to as many as 2 ½ Teaching Elders to 1 Ruling Elder (2.5 to 1) at some Assemblies; and, Whereas, the rising costs associated with attending GA has become a burden for many churches; therefore they send only one Commissioner and he is the pastor; and, Whereas, the perception of congregations is that General Assembly is a time for Teaching Elders to renew friendships rather than being on the floor and participating in the business of the Church; and, Whereas, Ruling Elders do not have the advantage of knowing others attending; in comparison to Teaching Elders who have developed friendships in seminary and other ministry opportunities; and, Whereas, many of the Ruling Elders are frustrated by parliamentary, maneuvering and/or debate over semantics and technical points; and, Whereas, many Ruling Elders subsequently become discouraged and choose not to return; and, Whereas, the expenses for Ruling Elders are greater than the Teaching Elders (i.e. the RE often must use vacation and/or personal time; some self-employed are required to hire a substitute for them [increasing their personal cost]; and others often pay all the expenses related to GA from their personal account); and, Whereas, previous assemblies (M16GA, p. 211) instructed the Admi

“Publish Hardback Version and Newly Edited Versions of Westminster Standards” Whereas, the Westminster Standard [sic] (hereafter called Standards) [Standards] are [part of] the Constitution of the Presbyterian Church in America (hereafter called the PCA); and Whereas, the Standards [Standards] are the most complete and accurate of any doctrinal statement available to the church today; and Whereas, the Standards [Standards] ought to be used widely without distraction; and Whereas, the current published PCA version of the Standards [Standards], 1990, contain[s] numerous typographical errors, omissions and other mistakes both in the text and in the citations which are a distraction to those who use it; and Whereas, this same published version of the Standard [sic] [Standards] is available mostly in a paper bound edition that does not remain intact with much serious use; Therefore, Let It Be Resolved, that the P C A [sic] is instructed [sic] to publish a corrected [sic] edition of the version of the Standards [Standards] in a durable, and reasonably priced edition. Approved by Heritage Presbytery on January 27, 2001. Attested by: /s/ Bruce Howes, Stated Clerk 5. source: Westminster Presbytery listed three requests for
body: action: 1) “To instruct the PCAF that it must allow members of the General Assembly to inspect the records in accord with the denominational corporation charter and Delaware state law;” 2) “To instruct all of the committees and agencies, including those that may be separately incorporated, of the rights of the members of the General Assembly in this regard;” 3) “That if (the General Assembly) is unable to assert its rightful ownership of and oversight over these agencies, the Assembly begin the process of divesting itself of these agencies.” Grounds: Request 1 [see text below]: 1. The PCAF is fully under and submissive to the General Assembly in all the provisions of the BCO, RAO and its own by-laws and articles of incorporation. 2. This authority and submission is implemented by the Board of Trustees elected at-large by the General Assembly and the work of the Committee of Commissioners and their report to the General Assembly and the subsequent actions of the GA. 3. The Overture originates from a request for information that was denied by the PCAF Board of Trustees in accordance with the General Assembly approved “Confidentiality Policy.” 4. The Overture cites Delaware Civil law; however, the PCAF is incorporated under Georgia law. 5. The Overture appears to use civil law to circumvent the ecclesiastical government of the General Assembly over the PCAF and other Committees and Agencies. body: “Allow Inspection of PCAF and All Other Committee and Agency Records by Individuals or General Assembly Divest Itself of Committees and Agencies” Whereas, the PCA Foundation was formed by the General Assembly of the Presbyterian Church in America; and Whereas, the PCA Foundation is a constituent part of the Presbyterian Church in America, and all of the members of the PCA Foundation Board are elected by the General Assembly of the Presbyterian Church in America (Corporate Bylaws of Presbyterian Church in America (A Corporation), Article VI.5); Whereas, the intimate connection between the Foundation and the denomination is seen even in the ability of the Board of the Foundation to designate a member of the Administrative Committee of the General Assembly (,BCO 14-1-12); and Whereas, the PCA Foundation, which was originally a subcommittee, gained separate agency status in 1983, similar to other agencies, such as Covenant College, Covenant Theological Seminary, Ridge Haven, and Insurance, Annuities, and Relief (M9GA, p. 116, 8.e; Ml 1GA, p. 113, 1.); and Whereas, the provisions regarding Covenant College and Covenant Theological Seminary in 1983 included the following: “Since the General Assembly is responsible for the conduct of the work which the whole church does unitedly, the powers and activities that it has delegated to institutions under its jurisdiction shall always be subject to review. It shall not delegate any of its general powers beyond its own recall so as to cease to be fully responsible for the spiritual oversight of the entire denomination” (M9GA, p, 100, 5.i)

body: Westminster Standards ” It is the opinion of the CCB that the proposed constitutional language creates constitutional conflicts with the BCO Preliminary Principles. Furthermore, the proposed language creates constitutional ambiguity. 1. The proposed overture language “either in his private life” is in conflict with BCO Preliminary Principle II. 1 and II.7. 2. The proposed overture language requiring Presbyteries to record every man’s views disagreeing with the Constitution will erode the Presbyteries’ exclusive authority to determine if a candidate receives and adopts the Confession of Faith and the Catechism of the PCA as containing the system of doctrine taught in the Holy Scriptures. See BCO 21-5. This is in conflict with BCO Preliminary Principle II.2 that recognizes the right of each court to determine its membership. (BCO Preface II.2, BCO 21-1 through 7; BCO 13-1; BCO 13-9 a,c,d; BCO 11-3 & 4). 3. An ambiguity created by the proposed language is the introduction of the phrase: “exception to the standards.” This phrase is nowhere else found in our constitution. The phrase elicits a plethora of interpretations, many of which go beyond our present Constitutional parameters. 4. It has not normally been the mission of the CCB to decide on the placement of Constitutional language. [For text and action, see 30-50, III, 3, p. 238.] Adopted by CCB

Abolish the Standing Judicial Commission” It is the opinion of the CCB that this overture is in conflict with other parts of the constitution, including but not limited to BCO 14-1(15). source: Eastern Canada Presbytery: Add to BCO 26-1 Procedure
body: for dealing with alleged conflicts between scripture and the constitution of the PCA It is the opinion of the CCB that the Overture is in conflict with other parts of the Constitution. Specific areas of conflict include, but are not limited to: 1. The Overture would allow a General Assembly to avoid the provisions of the BCO without appropriate constitutional process and would result in serious constitutional uncertainty. (BCO 26-2). 2. The Overture allows modification of virtually any action of the General Assembly or commission thereof, with the sole limitation that an actual judgment of a judicial commission may not be changed or deleted, thereby undermining the very principle of orderly Church Government which the BCO is designed to protect. [See BCO Preface II.2], 3. The Overture is in conflict with BCO 15.1 and 15.5, in that it allows for modification of a commission report. 4. The Overture requires the CCB to propose business, inconsistent with RAO 7-2 and the Operations Manual for CCB. 5. Finally, reference is made to the recommendation of the CCB with respect to Constitutional Inquiry #4 of the 29th General Assembly: “The presumption that we need to suspend our rules in order to obey Christ is an opinion that the CCB is not willing to affirm. i four standards are shown to be out of accord with Scripture, then there are proper procedures to follow, at each level, in order to change those standards. ” [For text and action, see 30-53, III, 18, p. 273.] Adopted by CCB

source: Westminster Presbytery: Amend BCO 24-1 to Record All
body: Exceptions with Presbytery and Have Stated Clerk Report All Exceptions to the General Assembly ” It is the opinion of the CCB that Overture 29 from Westminster Presbytery is in conflict with the Constitution. Specifically: 1. it allows a presbytery to determine whether a man mayor may not teach what he believes, even if it is not out of accord with any fundamental of the system of doctrine,thus going beyond our constitution to bind the man’s conscience. (BCO Preface II.1 and II.7) 2. It is also unconstitutionally vague in not identifying what the prohibition to teach would restrict or allow. 3. The last paragraph unconstitutionally provides a mechanism that puts the final approval for presbytery membership at the GA instead of the presbytery level. (BCO Preface II.2, BCO 21-1 through 7, BCO 13-1; BCO 13-9a,c,d; BCO 11-3&4). [For text and action, see 30-50, III, 2, p. 218.] Adopted by CCB (TE Chapell recused himself)

[See Appendix A, p. 346.] V. Review of Minutes of the Standing Judicial Commission 1. SJC Minutes Nov. 29, 2001, p. 21 ff. Response: In accordance with SJC Manual 20.5, the CCB approves the minutes of the Nov. 29, 2001 meeting of the Standing Judicial Commission (SJC) as being in conformity to the Manual of the SJC and mo 15. Editorial notations regarding form (e.g., punctuation and format) were passed to the Stated Clerk. 2. SJC Minutes March 7, 2002, p. 31 ff. Response: We postpone action on the March 7, 2002 minutes of the SJC and respectfully request the SJC to request more time of the General Assembly and/or reconsider its action “to draft and present to the 30th General Assembly procedures to be placed in the SJC Manual for examining concurring and dissenting opinions to be sure that such opinions are in temperate language ...“ prior to the final report that the CCB makes to the GA. 3. Procedure for Hearing a Memorial Response: It is the advice of CCB that the “Procedure for Hearing a Memorial'’ raises significant constitutional issues. These issues include: 1) our concern that the procedures may reflect a definition of a memorial inconsistent with our historical Presbyterian usage since no clear definition is offered and the term has been variously used in other/former d

source: OVERTURE 22 from New River Presbytery (to MNA)
body: “Form Blue Ridge Presbytery” Whereas the churches of New River Presbytery located within the Commonwealth of Virginia have for some time prayed about, considered, and most recently held planning meetings (including several churches in James River and Potomac Presbyteries) concerning the establishment of a new Presbytery in western Virginia, and Whereas the demographic, sociological and economic connections of churches throughout western Virginia - principally along and to the west of the Blue Ridge Mountains - make working together as a Presbytery highly desirable, and Whereas the churches currently located in western Virginia have discovered that they are of one mind in their desire to do church planting in this region, as well as develop an enlarged work of Reformed University Ministries among the many colleges and universities in this region (in addition to the other normal functions of a Presbytery), and, Whereas, while the remaining churches located in West Virginia fall just short of the minimum membership and attendance goals, they have a special cohesiveness that their leadership believes will enable them to overcome their somewhat small size, Therefore, New River Presbytery, meeting at Nitro, West Virginia in its stated meeting of March 9th, 2002, hereby overtures the 30th General Assembly that, effective September 1, 2002 a new Presbytery of the Blue Ridge be formed by the division of New River Presbytery (along with churches from James River Presbytery and Potomac Presbytery - see separate overtures) as follows: a. New Boundaries of New River Presbytery: “All of

source: OVERTURE 8 from James River Presbytery (to MNA)
body: “Revise Boundaries of James River Presbytery” Whereas, having been appraised by formal overture of the desire of several of the churches in our Presbytery located in the western area of Virginia desiring to unite with the newly forming Presbytery of the Blue Ridge, and Whereas, having reviewed the proposed overture from New River Presbytery asking the General Assembly to establish this new Presbytery, and Whereas, having reviewed the proposed new boundaries of James River Presbytery contained in this proposed overture from New River Presbytery, Therefore be it resolved that James River Presbytery, voting at Hopewell, Virginia in the One Hundred and Fifth Stated Meeting of January 19, 2002 does hereby approve of the following change to our boundaries effective September 1, 2002: Revised boundaries of James River Presbytery will be as follows: “All of Virginia south and east of and including the following counties, except for the counties of Accomac and Northampton on the Eastern Shore: Northumberland, Westmoreland, King George, Stafford, Spotsylvania, Hanover, Goochland, Cumberland, Buckingham, Prince Edward, Charlotte, and Halifax.” 10. That the General Assembly re-elect James C. Pakala and TE Douglas E. Lee to another term on the Presbyterian and Reformed Joint Commission on Chaplains and Military Personnel (PRJC) as members of the Class of 2006. Adopted 11. The 2003 GA/MNA Budget is deferred to the Committee on Administration. Deferred to AC report [See 30-62, III, 7, p. 295.] 12. That we recommend to the GA that we defer action on Recommendation 12 of the Permanent Commi

source: OVERTURE 13 from Session of Faith Reformed Presbyterian
body: Church (to MNA) “Revise Boundaries of Proposed Blue Ridge Presbytery” Overture To New River Presbytery and the 30th General Assembly of the PCA from Faith Reformed Presbyterian Church, Fairmont, WV Whereas the New River Presbytery is in the process of a major change in that the churches located in Virginia have proposed the formation of a separate Presbytery as the Presbytery of Blue Ridge - and Whereas Allegheny and Garrett counties in Maryland are within the bounds of the Pittsburgh Presbytery - and Whereas the present churches in the northern part of West Virginia are much closer to the churches in the Pittsburgh Presbytery [than] they are to all the other churches in the New River Presbytery - and Whereas the closer proximity to these churches in the Pittsburgh Presbytery would give greater opportunity for fellowship and shared activities such as missions conferences, church planting efforts and participation in Presbytery meetings and Presbytery Committee responsibilities - and Whereas the travel time to attend Pittsburgh Presbytery meetings would be at least an hour less than required to attend meetings of the New River Presbytery in the Charleston area Therefore Be It Resolved that the northern most counties of West Virginia, namely, Preston, Marion, Monongalia, Wetzel (and those in the northern pan handle), Marshall, Ohio, Brook and Hancock be included within the boundary of the Pittsburgh Presbytery - and Be It Further Resolved that the present churches within these counties: Christ Community, Faith Reformed and Trinity be transferred to the Pittsburgh Presbytery a

source: OVERTURE 16 from Rocky Mountain Presbytery (to MNA)
body: “Redefine the Geographical Boundaries of Rocky Mountain Presbytery to Include the State of Montana” Whereas, Rocky Mountain Presbytery is already overseeing churches and missions in the great state of Montana; and Whereas, we are establishing additional churches in the state of Montana; and Whereas, the state of Montana is not in any other PCA Presbytery; Therefore, Rocky Mountain Presbytery respectfully overtures the General Assembly to add the state of Montana to the geographical bounds of Rocky Mountain Presbytery. 16. That Overture 23 from the New York State Presbytery to redraw New York State Presbytery Boundary be answered in the affirmative. Adopted [The substance of Communication 5 (text below) was dealt with, though not explicitly, by Assembly action on Overture 23.]

source: OVERTURE 23 from New York State Presbytery (to MNA)
body: “Redraw New York State Presbytery Boundary” Whereas, the current western boundary of the New York State Presbytery includes central New York east and including Monroe, Ontario, Livingston and Steuben counties; and, Whereas, the current eastern boundary of the Ascension Presbytery includes western New York west and including Orleans, Genesee, Wyoming, and Allegany counties; and, Whereas, the Presbyterian Church of Wellsville and its teaching elder desires to leave the Ascension Presbytery and affiliate with New York State Presbytery; and Whereas, the Presbyterian Church of Wellsville is located in Allegany county which is currently within the boundary of the Ascension Presbytery; and, Whereas, the New York State Presbytery desires to receive the Presbyterian Church of Wellsville and its teaching elder; and Whereas, the New York State Presbytery desires to initiate and oversee the planting of healthy and reproducing PCA churches in every major city in upstate New York including Buffalo; and Therefore, Be It Resolved that the New York State Presbytery hereby overtures the 30th General Assembly of the Presbyterian Church in America to redraw the western boundary of New York State Presbytery and the northeastern New York boundary of Ascension Presbytery, to wit, that the counties currently belonging to the Ascension Presbytery which are in New York State be transferred to New York State Presbytery, thus having all the churches and counties within the geographical bounds of New York State, excepting those churches and counties belonging to Metropolitan New York Presbytery, within

source: OVERTURE 19 from Chesapeake Presbytery (to B&O & AC)
body: “Erect a Study Committee on Ministry Amidst Ethnic Diversity” Whereas Christ calls us to penetrate cultural boundaries with the gospel, and consequently embrace the multi cultural fellowship our Lord creates, and Whereas our own nation has not escaped the ravages of racism and ethnic oppression that typically afflict our fallen world, and Whereas Chesapeake Presbytery adopted the following affirmations at its stated meeting on 16 February 2002 : Affirmations of Ethnic Diversity. 1. Evangelism of people from various racial backgrounds and social classes, with the accompanying reconciliation of such believers in the Body, is an essential element of the Church’s calling to preach the Gospel (Acts 15). 2. A faithful presentation of the gospel to the particular people groups in our region demands cultural sensitivity (1 Cor 9:19-23). 3. The gospel that reconciles Jews and Gentiles in the New Testament is the same gospel that reconciles all believers regardless of race and class. This work of reconciliation is not optional (Gal. 3:26-29; Eph. 2:14-18; Col. 3:11). 4. We must guard against superficial unity that is an affront to the dignity of God’s image bearers and damages an authentic presentation of the gospel (Acts 6:1-7). 5. Our leaders and church members need to be equipped in order to reach the diverse people of their changing cultural environment (Eph. 4:1-13). 6. We confess and repent of sins of disunity (racism, prejudice, injustice, ethnic superiority, bitterness, lack of forgiveness, cultural idolatries, etc.,) (Gal. 2:11; 1 Cor 11:17-22; James 2:1-7). 7. The church mu

source: OVERTURE 5 from Ohio Valley Presbytery (to CCB & B&O)
body: and Metropolitan New York Presbytery, Uliana Presbytery, Missouri Presbytery, Piedmont Triad Presbytery, North Georgia Presbytery, Evangel Presbytery, Fellowship Presbytery, Tennessee Valley Presbytery, Rocky Mountain Presbytery, North Texas Presbytery, Nashville Presbytery, Susquehanna Valley Presbytery, Northern California Presbytery, North Florida Presbytery, Heartland Presbytery, Central Florida Presbytery, Northern Illinois Presbytery, South Texas Presbytery and Southeast Alabama Presbytery “Require Ten Percent of Presbyteries to Assume Original Jurisdiction Over a Minister in BCO 34-1 - Version 1” Whereas, the grassroots history of the Presbyterian Church in America indicates her desire to act in unity with the consensus of her churches; and Whereas, the present wording of Book of Church Order 34-1 allows only two presbyteries to require the General Assembly to decide whether to assume original jurisdiction in judicial process against a minister in another Presbytery; and Whereas, this standard can allow presbyteries of a particular perspective, and potentially quite few churches, to challenge the ordinary rights of a Presbytery to determine its own membership according to the men and circumstances best known by the local Presbytery; and Whereas, this standard, in effect, can allow two presbyteries and a few churches, to bring charges against one or many presbyters across Presbytery boundaries and potentially to flood the General Assembly with charges that express the concerns of very few; and Whereas, such narrow concerns should not be allowed to challenge the ordina

source: OVERTURE 18 from Potomac Presbytery (to CCB & B&O)
body: “Amend Both BCO 33-1 and 34-1 to Require Greater Consensus to Assume Original Jurisdiction Over Members and Ministers” Whereas the history of the Presbyterian Church in America, with respect to the former denominational connection of many of its officers and congregations, indicates the great importance of the voice of a minority in attempts to preserve the faithfulness of a denomination; whereas the present wording of the Book of Church Order wisely makes provision for the correction of one court through the efforts of others of like grade, through an appeal to a more inclusive court; Whereas the grassroots history of the Presbyterian Church in America indicates her desire to act in unity with the consensus of her churches; whereas the present wording of Book of Church Order of 33- 1 allows two Sessions to require a Presbytery to assume original jurisdiction injudicial process against a member in another congregation, and BCO 34-1 allows two presbyteries to require the General Assembly to assume original jurisdiction in judicial process against a minister in another Presbytery; Whereas, as the Presbyterian Church in America grows, this standard, expressed in a fixed number, will allow an increasingly smaller representation of the denomination to challenge the ordinary rights of a Session or Presbytery to fulfill its Constitutional duties; Whereas the ordinary rights of a court to fulfill its constitutional responsibilities should not be liable to challenge unless there is a significant proportion in the church that is persuaded that a court has failed to act properly; and

source: OVERTURE 30 from Philadelphia Presbytery (to CCB & B&O)
body: and Iowa Presbytery “Require Ten Percent of Presbyteries to Assume Original Jurisdiction Over a Minister in BCO 34-1 - Version 2” Whereas, the grassroots history of the Presbyterian Church in America indicates her desire to act in unity with the consensus of her churches; and Whereas, the present wording of Book of Church Order 34-1 allows only two presbyteries to require the General Assembly to decide whether to assume original jurisdiction in judicial process against a minister in another Presbytery; and Whereas, this standard can allow presbyteries of a particular perspective, and potentially quite few churches, to challenge the ordinary rights of a Presbytery to determine its own membership according to the men and circumstances best known by the local Presbytery; and Whereas, this standard, in effect, can allow two presbyteries and a few churches, to bring charges against one or many presbyters across Presbytery boundaries and flood the General Assembly with charges that express the concerns of very few; and Whereas, such concerns should not be allowed to challenge the ordinary rights of any Presbytery to determine its own membership unless there is a wide consensus in the church that a Presbytery has failed to act properly; Therefore, to ensure that a small minority of presbyteries or churches does not exercise inordinate influence over other churches, presbyteries and the General Assembly in pursuing the discipline of ministers; and to ensure that there is a significant indication of a likely consensus in the denomination that the GA must assume original jurisdiction

Faith Subscription - Version 5”), Overture 29 from Westminster Presbytery (“Amend BCO 24-1 to Record All Exceptions with Presbytery and Have Stated Clerk Report All Exceptions to the General Assembly”), Overture 31 from Iowa Presbytery (“Specify Good Faith Subscription - Version 6”), and Overture 32 from Philadelphia Presbytery (“Specify Good Faith Subscription - Version 7”) be answered by reference to the Assembly’s action on Overture 10. [See also 30-29, III, p. 99 ff.] Adopted

source: OVERTURE 10 from Ohio Valley Presbytery (to CCB & B&O)
body: “Specify Good Faith Subscription - Version 2” Whereas, the purity and unity of our church require her to establish that her officers sincerely receive and adopt the Westminster Standards as containing the system of doctrine taught in the Holy Scriptures, and for those officers to declare if at any time they find themselves out of accord with any of the fundamentals of this system (BCO 21-5; WCF XXXI.2); and Whereas, the purity and unity of the church also require that her leaders understand both the restrictions and allowances of her Standards that are derived from Scripture but, unlike it, are not the only infallible rule of faith and practice (WCF 1.8, 10; XX.2; XXXI.3; WLC # 3; BCO Preface II. 7); and Whereas, there is a longstanding tradition within American Presbyterianism, including the PCA, that officers may in good faith take exception to certain particulars of the Westminster Standards, if such particular exceptions are not inimical (i.e. hostile or injurious) to the more comprehensive system of doctrine; and Whereas, the PCA’s original Good-faith subscription position has served the church well, but recently been challenged by some who desire either a more Broad- or Strict-subscription position; and Whereas, the church must guard against a broad, “substance- of-doctrine” subscription approach, which has no definitive meaning and leaves to the candidate himself rather than to the wisdom of the Presbytery to determine what areas of his disagreement with particular statements of the Constitution are inimical to the system of doctrine and what are allowable difference

source: OVERTURE 24 from North Texas Presbytery (to CCB & B&O)
body: “Specify Good Faith Subscription - Version 5” In order to clarify and support the original ordination standards of the PCA, North Texas Presbytery overtures the General Assembly to amend the Book of Church Order by inserting the paragraphs in bold below as new paragraphs of BCO 21-4 between the following sentences, as indicated: “Whenever a Presbytery shall omit any of these parts, it shall always make a record of the reasons for such omissions and of the trial parts omitted. [Insert new paragraphs here] “The Presbytery being fully satisfied of his qualifications for the sacred office....” While our Constitution does not require the candidate’s affirmation of every statement and/or proposition of doctrine in our Confession of Faith and Catechisms, it is the right and responsibility of the Presbytery to determine if the candidate is out of accord with any of the fundamentals of these doctrinal standards and, as a consequence, may not be able in good faith sincerely to receive and adopt the Confession of Faith and Catechisms of this Church as containing the system of doctrine taught in the Holy Scriptures (cf. BCO 21-5, Q. 2; 24-5, Q.2). Therefore, in examining a candidate for ordination the Presbytery shall inquire not only into the candidate’s knowledge and views in the areas specified above, but also shall require the candidate to state the specific instances in which he may differ with the confession of faith and Catechisms in any of their statements and/or propositions. The court may only grant an exception to any difference of doctrine if in the court’s judgment the can

source: OVERTURE 29 from Westminster Presbytery (to CCB & B&O)
body: “Amend BCO 24-1 to Record All Exceptions with Presbytery and Have Stated Clerk Report All Exceptions to the General Assembly” Whereas, several of our sister presbyteries have overtured to amend the Book of Church Order with respect to the matter of doctrinal subscription; and Whereas, we, too, are desirous of promoting the purity, peace, and unity of the church; and Whereas, we believe that in order to do so, it is important to implement wording that the entire church can accept; Now therefore, be it resolved, that Westminster Presbytery hereby overtures the General Assembly of the Presbyterian Church in America to begin the process of Constitutional amendment by adopting the following and sending it down to the presbyteries for advice and consent: To insert, in BCO 21-4, the following language: It is the right and responsibility of the Presbytery, as the court of original jurisdiction, to determine if the candidate is out of accord with any of the statements of these doctrinal standards and, as a consequence, may not be able sincerely to receive and adopt the Confession of Faith and Catechisms of this Church as containing the system of doctrine taught in the Holy Scriptures (cf. BCO 21-5, Q. 2; 24-5, Q.2). In examining a candidate for ordination the Presbytery shall inquire not only into the candidate’s knowledge and views in the areas specified above, but also shall require the candidate to state the specific instances in which he may differ with the Confession of Faith and Catechisms in any of their statements and/or propositions. source: OVERTURE 4 from Ohio Valley Presbytery (to CCB & B&O)
body: “Handling Exceptions to the Westminster Standards” Add two new paragraphs in BCO 21-4 where deemed appropriate by the Committee on Constitutional Business: “When Presbytery allows a man to hold an exception to the standards, it shall also determine the man may hold the exception in belief only, whether he may teach it, and whether he may practice it either in his private life or in his public ministry all so long as he does not disturb the peace and purity of the church.” “The Stated Clerk of Presbytery shall cause the man’s allowed exception(s) stated in his own words, along with the Presbytery’s action on the exception(s), to be recorded along with the ministerial obligation he is required to sign. This document will be attached to the minutes of Presbytery as an appendix.” 4. That the General Assembly allow the Committee to give a partial report on Overture 17 from Eastern Canada Presbytery early in the Assembly. Adopted Grounds: Overture 17 [see text below in Recommendation 5] proposes a change in the RAO. Even though we are recommending that the Overture be answered in the negative, we believe the Overture and our recommendation should be presented early in the Assembly so the Overture has a fair chance of being considered under RAO XVIII. 5. That Overture 17 from Eastern Canada Presbytery (“Eliminate Floor Nominations Against Specific Nominees; Change to Random Contests”) be answered in the negative. Adopted Grounds: Though such a usage might be abused, the RAO ought not, on this ground, restrict the rights of commissioners to nominate as they see best. There is nothi

source: OVERTURE 17 from Eastern Canada Presbytery (to B&O)
body: “Eliminate Floor Nominations Against Specific Nominees; Change to Random Contests” Whereas it has been alleged that nominations from the floor of General Assembly are being placed in a party spirit, not just to put desirable candidates before the Assembly, but to prevent particular individuals who do not pass some litmus test from being selected for committees of the General Assembly; and Whereas God’s people should avoid even the appearance of wrongdoing (1 Thess. 5:22; Mt 17:26-27; 1 Cor 10:32,33; 2 Cor 6:3); and Whereas random matching of floor nominees to committee nominees for elections would both make it impossible to manipulate the vote by matching a popular candidate against one who was deemed unacceptable, and also make it clear to all that this was not happening; Therefore The Presbytery of Eastern Canada overtures the 30th General Assembly to amend RAO 7-4.i by adding “Nominations from the floor shall be matched by the Nominating Committee by lot, first to any slots for which the Committee presented no nominee, and then to one of the slots for which there is a single nominee (including those filled by nominations from the floor), then to slots for which there are more than one nominee.” 30-51 Theological Examining Committee By unanimous consent, the Assembly determined to take up this report before proceeding to the Special Order report of the Nominating Committee. RE Bill Cranford, Chairman, led the Assembly in prayer and presented the report, which was received as information. The Theological Examining Committee (TEC) met twice from GA 2001 to June 2002. The ch

source: OVERTURE 1 from Western Carolina Presbytery (to CCB & B&O)
body: “Add ‘and unity’ to Sixth Ordination Vow in BCO 21-5” Whereas, the theme of the 29th General Assembly was “Unity of the Spirit in the Bond of Peace”; and Whereas, in Ephesians 4:3 Paul commands the people of God to “Make every effort to keep the unity of the Spirit through the bond of peace,” going on to declare that Christ has specifically given pastors and teachers to the Church so that the body of Christ may reach “unity in the faith (vs. 13); and Whereas, the sixth question proposed to Ruling Elders and Deacons at their ordination and installation calls them to promise to strive for the purity, peace, and unity of the Church (Book of Church Order 24-5): Therefore Be It Resolved, that the Western Carolina Presbytery petition the General Assembly to amend the Questions for Ordination of Teaching Elders found in Book of Church Order 21-5, by adding the words “and unity” to the sixth question, so that the question would now read: “Do you promise to be zealous and faithful in maintaining the truths of the Gospel and the purity and peace and unity of the Church, whatever persecution or opposition may arise unto you on that account?” 7. That Overture 2 from Western Carolina and Metropolitan New York Presbyteries (“Diversity of views on women in Military and Dismiss WIM Committee with Thanks - Version 1”) be answered in the negative and that Overture 21 from New River Presbytery (“Mutual Forbearance on Women in Military and Dismiss Women in Military Committee With Thanks”) and Overture 26 from Potomac Presbytery (“Diversity of Views on Women In Military and Dismiss WIM Committe

body: and Metropolitan New York Presbytery Military & B&O) “Diversity of Views on Women In Military and Dismiss WIM Committee with Thanks - Version 1” Whereas “it pleased the Lord, at sundry times, and in divers manner, to reveal Himself, and to declare that His will unto His Church; and afterwards, for the better preserving and propagating of the truth, and for the more sure establishment and comfort of the Church against the corruption of the flesh, and the malice of Satan and of the world, to commit the same wholly unto writing: which maketh the Holy Scripture to be most necessary; those former ways of God’s revealing His will unto His people being now ceased (WCF 1.1);” and Whereas “The whole counsel of God concerning all things necessary for His own glory, man’s salvation, faith and life, is either expressly set down in Scripture, or by good and necessary consequence may be deduced from Scripture: unto which nothing at any time is to be added, whether by new revelations of the Spirit or traditions of men (WCF 1.6);” and Whereas “God alone is Lord of the conscience, and hath left it free from the doctrines and commandments of men, which are, in any thing, contrary to His Word; or beside it, if matters of faith, or worship. So that, to believe such doctrines, or to obey such commands, out of conscience, is to betray true liberty of conscience (WCF 20.2);” and Whereas “Synods and councils are to handle, or conclude nothing, but that which is ecclesiastical: and are not to intermeddle with civil affairs which concern the commonwealth, unless by way of humble petition in cases ex

source: OVERTURE 21 from New River Presbytery (to Women in
body: “Mutual Forbearance on Women in Military Military & B&O) and Dismiss Women in Military Committee With Thanks” Whereas PCA members facing such concerns require sound pastoral counsel as regards their duty before God; and Whereas “The duty which God requireth of man, is obedience to his revealed will (WSC 39);” and Whereas “The moral law is summarily comprehended in the Ten Commandments (WSC 41);” and Whereas “The fifth commandment requireth the preserving the honor, and performing the duties, belonging to every one in their several places and relations, as superiors, inferiors, or equals (WSC 64);” and, “The fifth commandment forbiddeth the neglecting of, or doing any thing against, the honor and duty which belongeth to every one in their several places and relations (WSC 65);” and Whereas concerns related to women serving in the military touch upon the various responsibilities of superiors, inferiors and equals in the military chain of command and among the citizenry of the commonwealth; and Whereas “The sixth commandment requireth all lawful endeavors to preserve our own life, and the life of others (WSC 68);” and “The sixth commandment forbiddeth the taking away of our own life, or the life of our neighbor unjustly, or whatsoever tendeth thereunto (WSC 69);” Whereas concerns related to woman serving in the military understandably may involve concerns over potential increased loss of life due to reduced combat effectiveness with mixed sex troop arrangements; and Whereas “The seventh commandment requireth the preservation of our own and our neighbor’s chastity, in heart, sp

source: OVERTURE 26 from Potomac Presbytery (to Women in
body: “Diversity of Views on Women In Military Military & B&O) and Dismiss WIM Committee with Thanks - Version 2” Whereas, “it pleased the Lord, at sundry times, and in divers manner, to reveal Himself, and to declare that His will unto His Church; and afterwards, for the better preserving and propagating of the truth, and for the more sure establishment and comfort of the Church against the corruption of the flesh, and the malice of Satan and of the world, to commit the same wholly unto writing: which maketh the Holy Scripture to be most necessary; those former ways of God’s revealing His will unto His people being now ceased (WCF 1.1);” and Whereas, “The whole counsel of God concerning all things necessary for His own glory, man’s salvation, faith and life, is either expressly set down in Scripture, or by good and necessary consequence may be deduced from Scripture: unto which nothing at any time is to be added, whether by new revelations of the Spirit or traditions of men (WCF 1.6);” and Whereas, “God alone is Lord of the conscience, and hath left it free from the doctrines and commandments of men, which are, in any thing, contrary to His Word; or beside it, if matters of faith, or worship. So that, to believe such doctrines, or to obey such commands, out of conscience, is to betray true liberty of conscience (WCF 20.2);” and Whereas, “Synods and councils are to handle, or conclude nothing, but that which is ecclesiastical: and are not to intermeddle with civil affairs which concern the commonwealth, unless by way of humble petition in cases extraordinary; or, by way of advice

source: OVERTURE 6 from Westminster Presbytery (to CCB & B&O)
body: “Revise BCO 15-4 and 15-5 to Abolish the Standing Judicial Commission” Whereas, the Standing Judicial Commission (SJC) was established for the noble goal of seeking to guarantee consistency of Constitutional interpretation; and Whereas, throughout its relatively brief history the SJC has been the object of serious criticism of its procedures, policies, and judicial conclusions; and Whereas, the seriousness of earlier criticism led to the appointment of an Ad Interim Committee on Judicial Procedures (AICJP), which gave its final report at the 1996 General Assembly, and whose recommendations were adopted by the denomination; and Whereas, we believe that it is time to re-visit the question of the wisdom of having a Standing Judicial Commission; and Whereas, among the abuses of the system which we have recently witnessed include the admittance to record of statements by members of the SJC which clearly crossed the bounds of propriety; and Whereas, a significant portion of the 29th General Assembly clearly was frustrated by the inability to rectify the situation; and Whereas, under our present rules, there are no Assembly- wide checks and balances on the SJC; and Whereas, placing that much power in the hands of a relatively few individuals is not wise; and Whereas, there is increasing frustration among many in the PCA arising from a perception that the decisions of the SJC do not reflect predictable justice; and Whereas, we believe there is a better, more economical, and more efficient way of adjudicating cases; Now therefore, be it resolved, that Westminster Presbytery hereby o

“Forbid Freemasons from Being Officers in PCA” Whereas, “It belongeth to synods and councils, ministerially to determine controversies of faith, and cases of conscience; to set down rules and directions for the better ordering of the public worship of God, and government of His Church...” WCF 31:2. Whereas, “Our blessed Saviour, for the edification of the visible Church, which is His body, has appointed officers not only to preach the Gospel and administer the Sacraments, but also to exercise discipline for the preservation both of truth and duty.” Preface to the Book of Church Order, Preliminary Principles, paragraph 3. Whereas, the 13th General Assembly of the PCA in 1985 appointed a study committee to examine Freemasonry and whether its tenets were contrary to basic Christianity. M13GA, p. 133. Whereas, the 15th General Assembly of the PCA in 1987 received as evidence and for study and education of the entire membership of the PCA the report (M15GA, p. 464, et seq.) and accompanying documents from the study committee on Freemasonry and “exhort[ed] all members of Freemasonry and those considering membership in Freemasonry, who are also members of the PCA, to reconsider their relationship with that organization in the light of the evidence given in th[at] report and the Topical Summary of the Document Sources and particularly in light of such passages as 1 Thessalonians 5:21,22 and Matthew 6:22-24.” M15GA, p. 162. Whereas, the PCA study committee on Freemasonry found the following: - **a)** “joining the Masons requires actions and vows out of accord with Scripture.” - **b)

source: OVERTURE 14 from Rocky Mountain Presbytery (to CCB & B&O)
body: “Amend BCO 24-1 to Replace “should” With “shall” Whereas the BCO 24-1, line 5, states that a “...prospective officer should be an active male member...” using the subjunctive mood to indicate a likely rather than an expected case; and Whereas the normal circumstance for prospective officers must be an active and committed male member in a local PCA congregation; and Whereas the indicative mood “shall” would better express the norm for prospective candidates; Therefore the Rocky Mountain Presbytery, meeting April 26, 2001, respectfully overtures the General Assembly to replace the word “should” in line 5 of BCO 24-1 with the word “shall”. The new reading would then be keeping in mind that each prospective officer shall be an active male member...” 11. That Overture 15 from Rocky Mountain Presbytery (“Amend BCO 24-1 to Specify “men” Rather Than “persons”) be answered in the negative. [See also 30-29, III, p. 101.] Adopted Grounds: In this context “persons” refers to men. BCO 7-2 states “...these offices are open to men only.” BCO 24-1 provides, “...keeping in mind that each prospective officer should be an active male member who meets the qualifications set forth in I Timothy 3 and Titus 1.” It is worth noting that the term in question has been in this place since the original constitution of the PCUSA in 1788, so there can ben o suggestion of unwholesome contemporary influence in its employment.

source: OVERTURE 15 from Rocky Mountain Presbytery (to CCB & B&O)
body: “Amend BCO 24-1 to Specify “men” Rather Than “persons” Whereas, the Bible makes clear that the office of elder is for men only (see I Tim 2:12, I Tim 3:2 and Titus 1:6 [husband of one wife]) and Whereas, the PCA has consistently and unashamedly held this view and Whereas, while there may be some Biblical and exegetical disagreement about the ordination of women to the office of deacon among some in the PCA; yet at present the office of deacon is also available only to men (see I Timothy 3:2 [husband of one wife]) and Whereas, the BCO 24-1, line one, says “persons” which is gender neutral rather than men; Therefore Rocky Mountain Presbytery, meeting on April 26, 2001, respectfully overtures the General Assembly to replace the word “persons” in line one of BCO 24-1, with word “men”. The new reading would then be “Every church shall elect men to the offices of ruling elder and deacon ...” 12. That the General Assembly refer Overture 19 from Chesapeake Presbytery (“Erect a Study Committee on Ministry Amidst Ethnic Diversity”) to the Committee of Commissioners on Mission to North America for their recommendation to the General Assembly. [For text and action, see 30-31, III, 18, p. 188.] 13. That the General Assembly pause for prayer before beginning consideration of Overture 20, [see text and action at 30-53, III, 14, p. 262] and that the General Assembly suspend RAO 17- 4,d to allow 30 minutes on the main motion, without a motion for the previous question being in order, after the Committee, through its appointed representatives, has the opportunity to speak in favor of the Com

“Racial Reconciliation” Whereas, the Scriptures portray a covenantal pattern of both celebration of our rich heritage and repentance for the sins of our fathers; and, Whereas, our nation has been blessed even as we have repeatedly addressed iniquity, redressed injustice, and assessed restitution for our inconsistent application of the ideals of truth and freedom; and, Whereas, the heinous sins attendant with unbiblical forms of servitude—including oppression, racism, exploitation, manstealing, and chattel slavery—remain among the defining features of our national history; and, Whereas, the issues surrounding that part of our history continue to shape our national life, even creating barriers between brothers and sisters of different races and/or economic spheres from enjoying unencumbered Christian fellowship with one another; and, Whereas, the aftereffects of that part of our history continue to be felt in the economic, cultural, and social affairs of the communities in which we live and minister; We therefore confess our covenantal involvement in these national sins. As a people, both we and our fathers have failed to keep the commandments, the statutes, and the laws our God has commanded. We therefore publicly repent of our pride, our complacency, and our complicity. Furthermore, we seek the forgiveness of our brothers and sisters for the reticence of our hearts, which has constrained us from acting swiftly in this matter. As a people, we pledge to work hard, in a manner consistent with the Gospel imperatives, for the encouragement of racial reconciliation, the establish

source: OVERTURE 25 from Ascension Presbytery (to CCB & B&O)
body: “Revise BCO 12-2, add 12-11, revise 25-6 and 15-1 Regarding Procedures to Be Used if a Session Cannot Function Or Needs to Be Augmented” Whereas, in God’s providence, some of the organized churches within the Presbyterian Church in America may find themselves without any qualified ruling elders (due to death, ill health, relocation, etc.); and Whereas, if there are no ruling elders there is no way for a Session to meet (BCO 12-1), call a congregational meeting (BCO 25-2); examine prospective officers (BCO 24-1); exercise formal church discipline; or carry out any of the duties of (BCO 12-5); and Whereas, many would argue that the implication of lack of a Session and the language of BCO 5 and 13-9 indicate that the only choices for a Presbytery in the face of the above situation are either to dissolve the church completely or to dissolve it and reconstitute it as a mission work; and Whereas, BCO 15-1 seems to provide another alternative when it speaks of a commission serving as an interim Session, but it makes no mention of who determines that there is to be an interim Session, how such a determination is made, or how the rights of the congregation are protected; and Whereas, the aforementioned options may not be either the most prudent or acceptable to the congregation; and Whereas, if a church has only one ruling elder and no teaching elder it can still be an organized church but would seem to be faced with the same problems as those facing a church with no ruling elders in that it does not have a Session (see BCO 12-1, para 2); and Whereas, BCO 12-1 states that where ther

source: OVERTURE 27 from Eastern Canada Presbytery (to CCB & B&O)
body: “Add to BCO 26-1 Procedure for Dealing with Alleged Conflicts Between Scripture and the Constitution of the PCA” Whereas, the 29th General Assembly found itself in the position that its rules compelled it to distribute in its permanent records material harmful to the reputation of certain people and groups; and Whereas, the Constitutional Business Committee reported to the 29th General Assembly that we had no option but to follow BCO, even if it appeared to conflict with Scripture, with the only remedy being to amend BCO after the fact to avoid such action in the future; and Whereas, this was not a matter of judicial process against these people; and Whereas, this is disobedience to God’s command, as our church understands it (“The ninth commandment requires ... the preserving and promoting of ... the good name of our neighbor, ... a charitable esteem of our neighbors; ... sorrowing for, and covering of their infirmities” [Larger Catechism 144] and forbids “... all prejudicing ... the good name of our neighbors, ... unnecessary discovering of infirmities ...” [Larger Catechism 145], based on Scriptures such as: Prov 17:9 “He who covers a transgression seeks love, but he who repeats a matter separates friends”; 1 Pet 4:8 “And above all things have fervent love for one another, for ‘love will cover a multitude of sins’”; Prov 10:12 “Hatred stirs up strife, but love covers all sins”; Prov 12:16 “A fool’s wrath is known at once, but a prudent man covers shame”); and Whereas, according to our confessional understanding, this would be an offence even if the statements should be t

“Reject SJC Manual Proposed Changes and Remove Memorials From SJC” Whereas, the 29th General Assembly sent down to the presbyteries for discussion and input several proposals regarding the procedures of the Standing Judicial Commission (SJC); and Whereas, Westminster Presbytery did assign to its Overtures & Judicial Business (O&JB) Committee the task of evaluating these proposed amendments to the SJC Manual; and Whereas, the O&JB Committee reported its findings and its recommendations that these proposals be voted down; and Whereas, we as a Presbytery believe that it is not wise or prudent to adopt these proposals; Now therefore, be it resolved, that Westminster Presbytery hereby overtures the 30th General Assembly not to approve the proposed amendments, and, for the benefit of the Assembly, hereby attaches the finding of the O&JB Committee to this overture; And be it further resolved, that Westminster Presbytery hereby overtures the 30th General Assembly to remove the matter of “Memorials” from the purview of the SJC, since memorials are properly handled by the Committee on Review of Presbytery Records, per Book of Church Order Chapter 40. 20. That Personal Resolution #4 be answered in the affirmative as amended; and that Overture 33 be answered by reference to the Assembly’s action with regard to Personal Resolution #4. Adopted Whereas, the PCA affirms the full authority and plenary, verbal inspiration of the original autographs of the Holy Scriptures (Mt. 5:18; 2 Tim. 3:16; 2 Pet. 1:21); and Whereas, the proper task of Bible translators is to accurately reproduce the ori

source: OVERTURE 33 from Central Carolina Presbytery (to B&O & AC)
body: “Erect Study Committee Regarding ‘Today’s New International Version of the Bible’” Whereas, the International Bible Society and Zondervan Publishing Company has published “Today’s New International Version” (TNIV) of the New Testament; And whereas the stated translation approach for the TNIV is to be “gender neutral;” And whereas this approach adds words and phrases that are not found in any of the Hebrew and Greek manuscripts of the Bible (see examples below); And whereas, many PCA churches use the 1984 NIV version for public worship, curriculum, and private devotional readings, And whereas many will confuse the NIV with the TNIV; Be it resolved that Central Carolina Presbytery overtures the 30th GA of the PCA to appoint a study committee with a budget of $5,000 to investigate the TNIV and to make recommendations concerning this version in the interest of the peace and purity of the Church at large and our own denomination in particular. Examples: Dr Wayne Grudem, Research Professor of Bible and Theology at Phoenix Seminary in Scottsdale, Ariz., and a past president of the Evangelical Theological Society, pointed out to WORLD Magazine some changed meanings in the TNTV New Testament: Compare NIV Hebrews 2:6 (What is man that you are mindful of him, the son of man that you care for him?) with TNIV Hebrews 2:6 (What are mere mortals that you are mindful of them. human beings that you care for them?). The TNIV mistranslates the singular Greek words huios ("son") and anthropos ("man"). It removes the connection of this phrase to Jesus, who called Himself "the Son of Man." Compa

“Counsel Assembly-Level Personnel Not to Participate in PPLN or Lose Posts” Whereas, denominational management personnel openly participated in and led a meeting of an organization billing itself as “Presbyterian Pastoral Leadership Network (PPLN)” on April 10, 2002; and Whereas, participation by denominational management personnel in such a group is in apparent violation of the Conflict of Interest Policy adopted by the General Assembly (M21GA, pp. 174-77); and Whereas, although the Conflict of Interest Policy was adopted in light of questions of propriety with regard to financial transactions, the Policy itself is quite broad, and includes involvement with non-profit organizations; and Whereas, among the examples of conflict of interest are the following: “Using the PCA’s personnel, equipment, supplies, or good will for other than formally approved activities, programs, and purposes” and “Expending unreasonable staff time during normal business hours for ... other organization to the detriment of work performance for the PCA”; and Whereas, assistants to one or more management personnel have been used to create a website, to e-mail others, and to assist in the April 10, 2002 meeting; and Whereas, the purposes of this organization include the violation of the regulative principle of worship and the Presbyterian ideal of uniformity of worship as evidenced in the PPLN’s “Our Vision” viz. “We want our church’s future to be characterized by the following Gospel priorities ... “Ask AC to Provide Options for Lower Costs and Greater RE Participation at General Assembly” Whereas, the PCA has from its inception subscribed to the parity of Elders, and, Whereas, since the first GA in 1973 when the enrollment was 208 Ruling Elders and 179 Teaching Elders, the disparity, between Ruling Elders and Teaching Elders has grown to as many as 2 Vi Teaching Elders to 1 Ruling Elder (2.5 to 1) at some Assemblies; and, Whereas, the rising costs associated with attending GA has become a burden for many churches; therefore they send only one Commissioner and he is the pastor; and, Whereas, the perception of congregations is that General Assembly is a time for Teaching Elders to renew friendships rather than being on the floor and Participating in the business of the Church; and, Whereas, Ruling Elders do not have the advantage of knowing others attending; in comparison to Teaching Elders who have developed friendships in Seminary and other ministry opportunities; and, Whereas, many of the Ruling Elders are frustrated by parliamentary maneuvering and /or debate over semantics and technical points; and, Whereas, many Ruling Elders subsequently become discouraged and choose not to return; and, Whereas, the expenses for Ruling Elders are greater than the Teaching Elders (i.e. the RE often must use vacation and/or personal time; some self- employed are required to hire a substitute for them [increasing their personal cost]; and others often pay all the expenses related to GA from their personal account); and, Whereas, previous assemblies (M16GA, p. 211) instructed the Ad

I am writing to convey the greetings of the General Assembly of the Presbyterian Church in Western Australia to the General Assembly of the Presbyterian Church in America. Here in Western Australia we appreciate your partnership with us in the work of the gospel, especially as this is expressed in the presence amongst us of Rev. Stephen Young and his wife Sarah.
