Each nominee shall be examined in: - **a.** his Christian experience, especially his personal character and family management (based on the qualifications set out in 1 Timothy 3:1-7 and Titus 1:6-9), - **b.** his knowledge of Bible content, - **c.** his knowledge of the system of doctrine, government, discipline contained in the Constitution of the Presbyterian Church in America ( _BCO_ Preface III, The Constitution Defined), - **d.** the

source: OVERTURE 25 from the Northwest Georgia Presbytery (to CCB, OC)
body: “Amend _BCO_ 46-4 to Add a Class of Associate Membership for Honorably Retired TEs” **Whereas** our current _BCO_ 46-4 defines an associate member as those believers temporarily residing in a location other than their permanent homes; **Whereas** the aim of this overture is pastoral and not for the purpose of changing our polity; or the needs of TEs called to works which are not to a local church. (Those differing calls as far as a defined relationship to a local church require a more complex study); **Whereas** ministers of the gospel who have served the church well and received Honorably Retired (HR) status may need ongoing pastoral care and vital relationships that local churches can give but is not practical for Presbytery Shepherding committees or Pastoral Care Committee to implement. The pastoral care of an HR minister would be a good application of I Timothy 5:10 coupled with the charge of John 13:14 and more suitable for deacons and elders of a local church to tend to their needs, Paul’s Partnership with the Philippian church was vital to his wellbeing and sanctification. (Phil.1:1-4); **Whereas** we already have a provision to assign ministers under discipline to a local church ( _BCO_ 46-8). We hold to two types of discipline, one being the regular preaching of the Word, and the other which is discipline for the unrepentant/repenting brother or sister. The assigning of an HR TE as an Associate member of a local church for what we describe as good discipline and retaining membership in a Presbytery would give to an HR what the _WCF_ speaks of “so as to conduce to t

source: OVERTURE 26 from the Northwest Georgia Presbytery (to OC, AC)
body: “Erect Ad Interim Committee to Revise the Directory for Worship for Authoritative Use” **Whereas** even prior to the establishment of constitutional documents in American Presbyterianism, American Presbyterians utilized the Westminster Form of Government and Directory for Public Worship, as adopted by the Synod of Philadelphia in 1729, thereby affirming the historic practice of ordering worship according to biblical and confessional principles; **Whereas** all schisms within American Presbyterianism, including the Old Side–New Side division and the Old School–New School division, continued to use a Directory for Worship, demonstrating the enduring commitment to a biblically regulated and confessional approach to public worship; **Whereas** in 1789, the newly organized Presbyterian Church in the United States of America (PCUSA) formally adopted a revised Directory for Worship, which was continued in usage throughout subsequent denominational developments, including the formation of the Presbyterian Church in the Confederate States (PCCSA) in 1861 and the Presbyterian Church in the United States (PCUS) in 1865, and later maintained by the Presbyterian Church in America (PCA) as part of its _Book of Church Order_ in 1973; **Whereas** the _Westminster Confession of Faith_ explicitly affirms that the Church has the authority to establish a Directory for Worship, provided that it commands only what is taught in Scripture and applies Christian prudence and the light of nature to circumstances of worship, as stated in _Westminster Confession of Faith_ 1.6, 20.2, and 31.3; **Whereas

source: OVERTURE 27 from the Northwest Georgia Presbytery (to OC, AC, CC,
body: CDM, CTS, Geneva, MNA, MTW, PCAF, RH, RUF) “Erect Ad Interim Committee to Receive Suggestions for Permanent Committee and Agency Operations, Resources, and Expenses” **Whereas** every church and organization deserves loving, thoughtful, and wise review of its operations and expenses from time to time, and the highest levels of PCA committees and agencies have not had such in decades; and **Whereas** the proposal below has the goal and potential to bring greater unity and increasing support from churches and Presbyteries for the concerted work of all PCA units; and **Whereas** the PCA should desire to have growing and optimal support from the grassroots; and **Whereas** total receipts to all PCA agencies now exceed $200,000,000 annually (for which we give praise to God), and we wish to use all the Lord’s resources optimally, including possibly re-directing some of those funds back to the lower courts for vital ministry, such as church planting, revitalization, missions, supporting interns in smaller churches, etc., which may be a helpful step to unleashing Presbyteries and Sessions to reach their full potential with less overhead; and **Whereas** even if fears about well-intended reviews arise—turf protection may yet occur in fine churches—for the good of the church and its future improved stewardship, we commend this process, which we believe is fair, transparent, and at its heart providing maximum input from the grass roots to the General Assembly; and **Whereas** we may give thanks for each of our elected Coordinators and leaders, we are also blessed with many gifted serv

source: OVERTURE 28 from the Northwest Georgia Presbytery (to MNA)
body: “Demand Mission to North America (MNA) to Apologize and Terminate Personnel” **Whereas** in recent weeks certain employees and/or other representatives of Mission to North America (“MNA”) provided online counsel to undocumented persons on ways to avoid being detained by authorities of the United States Government; and **Whereas** in providing this information to undocumented persons the responsible employees and/or other representatives of MNA violated the following legal provisions: (1) Title 18 United States Code (U.S.C.) §2 paragraph (a), which states that “Whoever commits an offense against the United States or aids, abets, counsels, commands, induces or procures its commission, is punishable as a principal;” and (2) Title 8 U.S.C. § 1324 Paragraph (iv) which states that, “Any person who – encourages or induces an alien to come to, enter, or reside in the United States, knowing or in reckless disregard of the fact that such coming to, entry, or residence iso r will be in violation of law.” **Whereas** we find that appropriate action must be taken to remedy the unlawful conduct of the employees and/or other representatives of MNA who participated in or otherwise permitted the posting of the inappropriate information; and **Whereas** on February 12, 2025, MNA released a statement offering an apology for the “previously posted information” on their website which “erroneously allowed the posting of content that advised undocumented persons on ways to avoid being detained by authorities;” and **Whereas** we find the statement released by MNA on February 12, 2025, to be insuf

source: OVERTURE 29 from the Houston Metro Presbytery (to CCB, OC)
body: “Amend _BCO_ 15-5.c to Clarify the Handling of an SJC Minority Decision by GA” **Be it resolved** that _BCO_ 15-5.c be amended as follows. (Strike-through for deletions, underlining for new wording.) c. (1) If, within twenty-four (24) hours of the time of adjournment o fa standing judicial commission meeting at which a final decision was rendered in a case, at least one-third (1/3) of the voting members of the Standing Judicial Commission voting on a particular final decision file written notice of their intention to file a minority decision with the Stated Clerk of the General Assembly, and within twenty (20) days from the adjournment do file such a minority decision, such minority decision shall be considered a minority report and shall be referred, with the report of the Standing Judicial Commission, to the General Assembly. In each instance “file” shall be understood as defined by the _Operating Manual for Standing Judicial_ _Commission_ . (2) No such reference* from the Standing Judicial Commission shall be considered by the General Assembly unless the report of the Standing Judicial Commission, and the minority report, the Record of the Case, and all briefs timely filed by the parties have been distributed in electronic form mailed to the clerk of Session of each church and published in the _Commissioner Handbook_ at least thirty (30) days prior to the meeting of the General Assembly. (3) The Assembly shall act upon such a reference* from the Standing Judicial Commission, in each case without question, discussion, debate, or amendment, as follows: (a) The Standing Jud

body: CDM, CTS, Geneva, MNA, MTW, PCAF, RH, RUF) “Amend _BCO_ 8-4, 20-1, 21-1, 21-12, and 23-1 re the Calling and Dissolution of TE Relationships for Needful Works” **Be it resolved** that _BCO_ 8-4, 20-1, and 21-1 be amended as follows (underlining for additions): **8-4.** As the Lord has given different gifts to men and has committed to some special gifts and callings, the Church is authorized to call and appoint some to labor as teaching elders in such works as may be needful to the Church ( _cf_ . BCO 21-12). When a teaching elder is called to such needful work, it shall be incumbent upon him to make full proof of his ministry by disseminating the Gospel for the edification of the Church. He shall make a report to the Presbytery at least once each year. **20-1** . Before a candidate, or licentiate, can be ordained to the office of the ministry, he must receive a call to a definite work. Ordinarily the call must come from a church, Presbytery, a permanent Committee or Agency (see BCO 14-1.12), or the General Assembly of this denomination. If the call comes from another source, the Presbytery shall always make a record of the reasons why it considers the work t ob ea valid christian ministry. (See also BCO 8-7 and 21-1). A proper call must be written and in the hands of the Presbytery prior to being acted upon by a Presbytery. It must include financial arrangements (such as salary, vacation, insurance, retirement, etc.) between those calling and the one called, and assurance that the definite work will afford the liberty to proclaim and practice fully and freely the whole couns

source: OVERTURE 31 from the Houston Metro Presbytery (to CCB, OC)
body: “Amend _BCO_ 31-10 to Require Vote of the Entire Court to Suspend an Officer’s Official Functions during Process” **Be it resolved** that _BCO_ 31-10 is amended as follows (underlining for additions): **31-10** . when a member o fa church court i sunder process, all his official functions may be administratively suspended at the court’s discretion; but this shall never be done in the way of censure, and this requires a two-thirds (2/3) majority of the entire court, even if the process is being conducted by a commission. So that _BCO_ 31-10 as amended would read: **31-10** . when a member o fa church court i sunder process, all his official functions may be administratively suspended at the court’s discretion; but this shall never be done in the way of censure, and this requires a two-thirds (2/3) majority of the court itself, even if the process is being conducted by a commission. **RATIONALE:** There are occasions when a church officer (that is, “a member of a church court”) comes under process. Such process could potentially take a period of time to conclude. At the same time, it may be necessary for a number of reasons, including the honor of religion, the health of the court, and even the well-being of the accused, to suspend the accused from his official functions. Current _BCO_ 31-10 rightly points out that this is not a censure but is only an administrative suspension. Often process is conducted by a _BCO_ 15-3 commission rather than the entire court. Such a commission may be relatively small when compared to the entire court: a Presbytery commission must be at least

source: OVERTURE 32 from the Houston Metro Presbytery (to CCB, OC)
body: “Amend _BCO_ 36 to Provide for Elevation of a Censure without an Entirely New Trial” **Be it resolved** that _BCO_ 36 be amended to add a new paragraph, _BCO_ 36-8, as follows (underlining for additions): **36-8** . When members of a court wish to consider the elevation of an indefinite suspension, whether from office or from the Sacraments ( _BCO_ 36-5) to the censure of deposition or excommunication, it shall not be required to initiate process _de novo_ according to _BCO_ 31-2 or _BCO_ 32-2. The indefinitely suspended person is not in good standing and has already been found impenitent, resulting in indefinite suspension ( _BCO_ 30-3). However, the indefinitely suspended person has aright to a hearing in the matter. The court supervising the indefinite suspension may consider a motion to find him “incorrigible and contumacious” ( _cf_ . _BCO_ 30-4) and present evidence to that effect. The court shall give at least ten (10) days’ notice of such a motion to the indefinitely suspended person and permit him the privilege of the floor to plead and give a defense before the original trial court. The court, upon completing its hearing, shall vote to determine whether or not to elevate the indefinite suspension to deposition or excommunication. So that _BCO_ 36-8 as amended would read: **36-8** . When members of a court wish to consider the elevation of an indefinite suspension, whether from office or from the Sacraments ( _BCO_ 36-5) to the censure of deposition or excommunication, it shall not be required to initiate process _de novo_ according to _BCO_ 31-2 or _BCO_ 32-2. source: OVERTURE 33 from TE Fred Greco (to CCB, OC)
body: “Amend _BCO_ 57-2 re the Examination of Young Persons for Admission to the Sealing Ordinances” **Be it resolved** that _BCO_ 57-2 be amended as follows (underlining for additions): **57-2** . The time when young persons come to understand the Gospel cannot be precisely fixed. This must be left to the prudence of the Session, whose office it is to judge, after careful examination, the qualifications of those who apply for admission to sealing ordinances. The examination shall include an evaluation of the young person’s credible profession of faith, the young person’s understanding of the meaning of the Lord’s Supper, and the young person’s understanding of the questions in _BCO_ 57-5. So that _BCO_ 57-2 as amended would read: **57-2** . The time when young persons come to understand the Gospel cannot be precisely fixed. This must be left to the prudence of the Session, whose office it is to judge, after careful examination, the qualifications of those who apply for admission to sealing ordinances. The examination shall include an evaluation of the young person’s credible profession of faith, the young person’s understanding of the meaning of the Lord’s Supper, and the young person’s understanding of the questions in _BCO_ 57-5. **RATIONALE:** Current _BCO_ 57-2 properly highlights the fact that examining a young person for membership is an inexact matter. There is no mandatory age for admission to sealing ordinances. Sessions have discretion to use “prudence” in their examinations and the admission of young persons to sealing ordinances. source: OVERTURE 34 from the Savannah River Presbytery (to CCB, OC)
body: “Amend _RAO_ 16 to Require Reporting on Session and Diaconate Membership and Duties” **Whereas** the 45 [th] General Assembly of the Presbyterian Church in America adopted the 4th recommendation from the Study Committee on the Role of Women in the Ministry of the Church which, in its final form stated: “That sessions, if possible, establish a diaconate of qualified ordained men.”; and **Whereas** the rationale in the body of the report explaining this recommendation stated that “There is at least anecdotal evidence that some PCA churches are choosing not to establish an ordained diaconate, even with qualified candidates, because the church wishes to be free to establish a body of unordained servants, both male and female ( _Book of Church Order_ 9-7)”, and similar reports of this practice persist within our denomination; and **Whereas** _BCO_ 9-7 recognizes an unofficial and unordained role within the church called “assistants to the deacons” comprised of “godly men andS women of the congregation to assist the deacons in the caring for the sick, the widow, the orphans, the prisoners, and other who may be in distress or need”; and **Whereas** at its 50 [th] General Assembly, the PCA approved an amendment to the _Book of Church Order_ ( _BCO_ ) 7-3 and affirmed it at its 51 [st] General Assembly which prohibits assigning the titles connected to the ecclesial offices of pastor, elder, or deacon to non-ordained members; **Therefore be it resolved** the Savannah River Presbytery respectfully overtures the 52 [nd] General Assembly of the PCA to protect the constitutional practice

source: OVERTURE 35 from the Pacific Northwest Presbytery (to AC)
body: “Request AC to Study and Report on GA Meeting Locations” **Be it resolved,** the 52 [nd] General Assembly (GA) requests the Administrative Committee (AC) to research and report to the 54 [th] GA on necessary considerations and best practices for selecting and affording appropriate host cities and venues, and the impact of location on broad commissioner attendance across the PCA. **Rationale** — We understand that in the post-COVID environment, the hospitality industry has experienced significant cost pressures, coinciding with historic highs in GA Commissioner attendance over the last four years. With growth comes the need for larger spaces. Therefore, it is important to determine the best locations for future GAs, considering several factors. For example, should we consider larger cities with hub airports or continue to gather ina mix of mid-size markets in the Southeast and elsewhere? Would commissioners prefer to meet, for example, in ATL every year? Additionally, the AC should evaluate how well we're representing the entire PCA at GA as the denomination continues to expand beyond the Southeast. This includes considering site selection across the country and its impact on reaching commissioner attendance that broadly represents the PCA. Two years ago, at the 50 [th] GA in Memphis, 38% of our 87 Presbyteries had between 0 and 4 Ruling Elder (RE) commissioners, totaling just 42 RE commissioners. This is the same number of REs who attended from Covenant Presbytery alone. Put another way, 38% of our Presbyteries supplied 6% of the RE commissioners. Last year, at the 51 [st]

source: OVERTURE 36 from the Georgia Foothills Presbytery (to MNA)
body: “Change the Boundaries of Georgia Foothills and Metro Atlanta Presbyteries” **Whereas** Lumpkin County, GA is the farthest northern point of Metro Atlanta Presbytery; and **Whereas** it lies geographically in the northeast Georgia mountains, near many churches within the current boundary of Georgia Foothills Presbytery; and **Whereas** Georgia Foothills Presbytery desires to start an RUF campus ministry at University of North Georgia located in the city of Dahlonega in Lumpkin County; and **Whereas** the Session of Creekstone Church, the only PCA church located in Lumpkin County, is agreeable to come into Georgia Foothills Presbytery; and **Whereas** the minutes of the 46 [th] General Assembly established the boundary of Georgia Foothills Presbytery to be the Georgia counties of Hall, Barrow, Jackson, Clarke, Oconee, Habersham, and Union counties; also the counties of Oglethorpe, Elbert, Madison, Hart, Franklin, Banks, White, Stephens, Rabun, and Towns; and Gwinnett county north of the defined boundaries, from east to west, of Highway 316, then Sugarloaf Parkway to Peachtree Industrial Boulevard, then an imaginary line westward from the intersection of Peachtree Industrial Boulevard and Sugarloaf Parkway to the Chattahoochee River; and **Whereas** the Minutes of the 46 [th] General Assembly established the boundary of Metro Atlanta Presbytery to be the Georgia counties of Dekalb, Fulton, Clayton, Cobb (East of I-75), Dawson, Lumpkin, Coweta, Heard, Spalding, Troup, Merriweather, Pike, Fayette, Forsyth, Rockdale, Newton, Henry, and Walton; and Gwinnett county south of the de

In judicial cases, the Session may elect one of its members to preside as moderator shall be a or invite a minister of the pres byte ry to which the church belongs to preside as moderator. In judicial cases, the Session may elect one of its members to preside as moderator or invite a minister of the Presbytery to which the church belongs to preside as moderator. **RATIONALE:** Sessions should have discretion to elect a moderator in judicial cases. Some Sessions may have ruling elders who are highly experienced in church discipline and cases of process, and they should have the liberty to elect such a ruling elder to preside. In instances where this is not the case, the revised provision retains the right of the Session to invite a minister of the Presbytery to p

source: OVERTURE 38 from the Southeast Alabama Presbytery (to CCB, OC)
body: “Amend _BCO_ 30-4 to Require a New Case with Process for Elevation of Indefinite Suspension to Excommunication” **Be it resolved** that _BCO_ 30-4 be amended as follows (underlining for additions): **30-4.** Excommunication is the excision of an offender from the communion of the Church. This censure is to be inflicted only on account of gross crime or heresy and when the offender shows himself incorrigible and contumacious. Elevating indefinite suspension from the Sacraments to excommunication shall require a new case of process establishing that the matter is a gross crime or heresy and that the offender has shown himself incorrigible and contumacious. The design of this censure is to operate on the offender as a means of reclaiming him, to deliver the church from the scandal of his offense, and to inspire all with fear by the example of his discipline. So that _BCO_ 30-4 as amended would read: **30-4.** Excommunication is the excision of an offender from the communion of the Church. This censure is to be inflicted only on account of gross crime or heresy and when the offender shows himself incorrigible and contumacious. Elevating indefinite suspension from the Sacraments to excommunication shall require a new case of process establishing that the matter is a gross crime or heresy and that the offender has shown himself incorrigible and contumacious. The design of this censure is to operate on the offender as a means of reclaiming him, to deliver the church from the scandal of his offense, and to inspire all with fear by the example of his discipline. **RATIONALE:** The S

source: OVERTURE 39 from the Southeast Alabama Presbytery (to CCB, OC)
body: “Amend _BCO_ 34-8 and add _BCO_ 33-5 to Clarify the Process for Elevating Suspension from Office to Deposition” **Be it resolved** that _BCO_ 34-8 be amended as follows (underlining for additions; strikethrough for deletions): **34-8.** A minister under indefinite suspension from his office or deposed for scandalous conduct shall not be restored, even on the deepest sorrow for his sin, until he shall exhibit for a considerable time such an eminently exemplary, humble and edifying life and testimony as shall heal the wound made by his scandal. A deposed minister shall in no case be restored until it shall appear that the general sentiment of the Church is strongly in his favor, and demands his restoration; and then only by the court inflicting the censure, or with that court’s consent. The removal of deposition requires a three-fourths (3/4) vote of the court inflicting the censure, or a three-fourths (3/4) vote of the court to which the majority of the original court delegates that authority. Likewise, if it shall appear in time that any minister suspended from office fails to satisfy the requirements of _BCO_ 34-8 and/or _BCO_ 37-8, Presbytery may depose him from office, a majority of three-quarters (3/4) being necessary for this purpose. In such a case, the clerk shall under the order of the Presbytery forthwith deliver to the minister concerned a written note that, at the next stated meeting, the question of his being so dealt with is to be considered. This notice shall distinctly state the grounds for this proceeding. The party thus notified shall be heard in his own de

source: OVERTURE 40 from the Southeast Alabama Presbytery (to CCB, OC)
body: “Amend _BCO_ 35-9 to Require Recording All Parts of a Trial” **Be it resolved** that the 52 [nd] General Assembly revise _BCO_ 35-9 as shown below (underlining for additions; strikethrough for deletions): **35-9.** All testimony parts of a trial other than deliberations shall be recorded (by transcription, audiotape, videotape, or some other electronic means) and witnesses informed of such prior to testifying. Such recording becomes part of the Record of the Case. However, in order to be referenced in written or oral briefs, such recording must be transcribed and the transcription authenticated by the trial court. The court of final appeal may assess the cost of transcription equitably among the parties So that _BCO_ 35-9 as amended would read: **35-9.** All parts of a trial other than deliberations shall be recorded (by transcription, audiotape, videotape, or some other electronic means) and witnesses informed of such prior to testifying. Such recording becomes part of the Record of the Case. However, in order to be referenced in written or oral briefs, such recording must be transcribed and the transcription authenticated by the trial court. The court of final appeal may assess the cost of transcription equitably among the parties **Rationale** 1. This clarifies, for example, that objections raised by the parties during a trial and the court's ruling on those objections, are recorded and available for transcription in the event of an appeal _Adopted by the Southeast Alabama Presbytery at its stated meeting, April 22,_ _2025_ _Attested by TE Kevin Corley, stated clerk_

source: OVERTURE 41 from the Calvary Presbytery (to AC)
body: “Direct the AC to Collect & Report Each Congregation’s Public Worship Service Times” **Whereas** the worship of God, and particularly the corporate public worship of God, is vital to the Christian faith ( _WLC_ 117); and, **Whereas** proper observance of the Fourth Commandment requires due diligence and preparation ( _WLC_ 121); and **Whereas** due diligence and preparation requires knowing when particular congregations will gather for public worship; and, **Whereas** the charge of keeping the Sabbath is more specially directed to superiors (WLC 118); and **Whereas** information about public worship service times are at present occasionally unavailable and often tedious for individuals to collect through publicly accessible channels; and, **Therefore, be it resolved** that the 52 [nd] General Assembly direct the Administrative Committee to collect and report each congregation’s public worship service times (inclusive of all stated worship services) on the church directory map available at https://www.pcaac.org/churchdirectory/. **Therefore, be it further resolved** that the 52 [nd] General Assembly encourage the Administrative Committee to seek the assistance of the several Presbyteries in this task. _Adopted by Calvary Presbytery at its stated meeting of April 24, 2025._ _Attested by /s/ RE Melton L. Duncan, Stated Clerk_

source: OVERTURE 42 from the Calvary Presbytery (to AC)
body: “Direct the Stated Clerk Not to Collect Statistical Data Pertaining to Age or Ethnicity” **Whereas** _RAO_ 4-11 authorizes the Administrative Committee to collect statistical information for the purpose of providing and publishing “guidelines for the Partnership Share Giving Program of the PCA, including the Ministry Ask” and for no other stated reason; and, **Whereas** _RAO_ 4-11 specifies that said “guidelines shall include statistical information expressed both in per capita giving terms and in proportional (percentage) giving terms to demonstrate the giving necessary to attain full funding for budgets of the General Assembly Committees and Agencies” and nothing more; and, **Whereas** the Office of the Stated Clerk, through a note contained ina box on the bottom of page 2 of the 2024 Annual Statistical Report Form, informed the congregations of the PCA that “two new line items” regarding “communicant member ethnicity and age” would appear “on the 2025 Annual Statistical Report Form” to be sent out for completion in 2026; and, **Whereas** the practical difficulties of collecting information on “communicant member ethnicity and age” are greater than perhaps the Stated Clerk’s Office has yet considered due to members’ reticence to share their ages or uncertain of how best to categorize their ethnicity, with many of our members being the children of interracial/multiethnic unions; and, **Whereas** the theological commitments of the PCA derived from Scripture and expressed in the PCA’s subordinate Standards and Constitute militate against the collection of data pertaining to

source: OVERTURE 43 from the Calvary Presbytery (to CCB, OC, AC)
body: “Amend _RAO_ 4-11 regarding Data on Age and Ethnicity” **Whereas** _RAO_ 4-11 authorizes the Administrative Committee to collect statistical information for the purpose of providing and publishing “guidelines for the Partnership Share Giving Program of the PCA, including the Ministry Ask” and for no other stated reason; and, **Whereas** _RAO_ 4-11 specifies that said “guidelines shall include statistical information expressed both in per capita giving terms and in proportional (percentage) giving terms to demonstrate the giving necessary to attain full funding for budgets of the General Assembly Committees and Agencies” and nothing more; and, **Whereas** the Office of the Stated Clerk, through a note contained ina box on the bottom of page 2 of the 2024 Annual Statistical Report Form, informed the congregations of the PCA that “two new line items” regarding “communicant member ethnicity and age” would appear “on the 2025 Annual Statistical Report Form” to be sent out for completion in 2026; and, **Whereas** the practical difficulties of collecting information on “communicant member ethnicity and age” are greater than perhaps the Stated Clerk’s Office has yet considered due to members’ reticence to share their ages or uncertain of how best to categorize their ethnicity, with many of our members being the children of interracial/multiethnic unions; and, **Whereas** the theological commitments of the PCA derived from Scripture and expressed in the PCA’s subordinate Standards and Constitute militate against the collection of data pertaining to ethnicity, for the PCA confesses w

source: OVERTURE 44 from the Pee Dee Presbytery (to AC)
body: “Transition byFaith to a Press-Release-Based Publication” **Whereas** byFaith magazine was established as the official publication of the Presbyterian Church in America (PCA) to serve as a voice for the denomination; and **Whereas** the PCA is committed to maintaining a clear distinction between denominational communications and editorial commentary, ensuring that official communications remain impartial and representative of the church as a whole; and **Whereas** the purpose of denominational communication should be to provide clear and accurate information regarding the actions, policies, and ministries of the PCA without editorializing or presenting particular viewpoints that may not reflect the consensus of the denomination (1 Corinthians 14:40); and **Whereas** byFaith magazine has, at times, functioned as more than a source of official denominational news, engaging in commentary and editorial perspectives that may not always represent the collective voice of the General Assembly ( _BCO_ 14-1.5; _WCF_ 31.3); and **Whereas** many churches and Presbyteries would benefit from an official PCA publication that focuses on disseminating essential information, press releases, and updates from ministries and Presbyteries in a way that prioritizes clarity, transparency, and denominational unity (Ephesians 4:3); and **Whereas** transitioning byFaith into a press-release-based platform would enhance trust in denominational communications by ensuring that its content remains neutral, factual, and reflective of the entire PCA rather than selective viewpoints; and **Whereas** such tr

source: OVERTURE 45 from the Hills and Plains Presbytery (to MNA)
body: “Change the Boundaries of Covenant and Hills & Plains Presbyteries” _**OVERTURE 21 WITHDRAWN BY COVENANT PRESBYTERY**_ **Whereas**, the general guidelines for dividing Presbyteries approved by the 41 [st] General Assembly of the PCA state “Presbytery boundaries should be such that her member churches have a common commitment to the region within their boundaries and a deep commitment to their shared responsibility to cover the region with the Gospel through evangelism and church planting.” **Whereas**, the same general guidelines for dividing presbyteries further state “A Presbytery should have regional cohesiveness. The Presbytery should have a geographical spread that is as reasonable as possible to facilitate planting churches, encouraging fellowship, participating together in shared ministry and conducting the necessary business of the Presbytery.” **Whereas**, the 16 [th] General Assembly of the PCA adopted guidelines for Presbytery boundaries stating, “A presbytery should have a radius of 2 1/2 hours maximum driving distance.” **Whereas**, Sebastian County, Arkansas, where Covenant Presbyterian Church of Fort Smith is located on the farthest western border of Covenant Presbytery and is adjacent to the border of Hills & Plains Presbytery, and **Whereas**, the Session of Covenant Presbyterian Church of Fort Smith wants to regularly attend Presbytery meetings but finds it difficult at times due to extended travel, and **Whereas**, the locations of the stated meetings of Hills & Plains Presbytery are much closer to Fort Smith than the stated meetings of Covenant Presbyter

source: OVERTURE 46 from the Metro Atlanta Presbytery (to MNA)
body: “Change the Boundaries of Georgia Foothills and Metro Atlanta Presbyteries” **Whereas** Lumpkin County, GA is the farthest northern point of Metro Atlanta Presbytery; and **Whereas** it lies geographically in the northeast Georgia mountains, near many churches within the current boundary of Georgia Foothills Presbytery; and **Whereas** Georgia Foothills Presbytery desires to start an RUF campus ministry at University of North Georgia located in the city of Dahlonega in Lumpkin County; and **Whereas** the Session of Creekstone Church, the only PCA church located in Lumpkin County, is agreeable to come into Georgia Foothills Presbytery; and **Whereas** the minutes of the 46 [th] General Assembly established the boundary of Georgia Foothills Presbytery to be the Georgia counties of Hall, Barrow, Jackson, Clarke, Oconee, Habersham, and Union counties; also the counties of Oglethorpe, Elbert, Madison, Hart, Franklin, Banks, White, Stephens, Rabun, and Towns; and Gwinnett county north of the defined boundaries, from east to west, of Highway 316, then Sugarloaf Parkway to Peachtree Industrial Boulevard, then an imaginary line westward from the intersection of Peachtree Industrial Boulevard and Sugarloaf Parkway to the Chattahoochee River; and **Whereas** the minutes of the 46 [th] General Assembly established the boundary of Metro Atlanta Presbytery to be the Georgia counties of Dekalb, Fulton, Clayton, Cobb (East of I-75), Dawson, Lumpkin, Coweta, Heard, Spalding, Troup, Merriweather, Pike, Fayette, Forsyth, Rockdale, Newton, Henry, and Walton; and Gwinnett county south of the de

source: OVERTURE 47 from the Great Lakes Presbytery (to OC, AC)
body: “Erect Ad Interim Committee on Christian Nationalism **Whereas**, there is a question whether those that hold to the Historic _WCF_ (1647) concerning the magistrate ( _WCF_ 23-3) need to take an exception because of differences from the version of the _WCF_ adopted by the PCA (1788); and **Whereas**, some in the PCA have labeled the historic _WCF_ ’s views concerning the magistrate as a form of Christian Nationalism; and **Whereas**, some also hold to a version of Christian Nationalism that they describe as Ethno-Nationalism, or label themselves as Heritage Americans; and **Whereas**, those that hold these teachings profess conformity to the Reformed Tradition; and **Whereas**, these have caused confusion, division, and dissension even among the congregants of PCA churches and affected PCA pastors and officers; and **Whereas**, church courts have the responsibility to determine questions of doctrine seriously, and in general to maintain truth and righteousness, condemning erroneous opinions which tend to bring injury to the eace, purity, or progress of the Church ( _BCO_ 11-4); **Therefore, be it resolved** that the 52 [nd] PCA General Assembly erect and convene an Ad Interim Study Committee on Christian Nationalism ( _RAO_ 9-1), to study the relationship between Christian Nationalism, Ethno-Nationalism, and related teachings. Further, the committee shall advise on whether these teachings and formulations are in conformity with the system of doctrine taught in the _Westminster_ _Standards_ or where they may diverge from the system of doctrine. **Further**, the committee sha

source: OVERTURE 48 from the Tennessee Valley Presbytery (to OC, AC)
body: “Erect an Ad Interim Committee for a Pastoral Letter on Christian Citizenship and Church-State Relations” **Whereas,** the duties of Christian citizenship are often disputed and prove perennially difficult for both elders and church members to discern; and **Whereas**, confusion exists about the proper relation between church and state; and **Whereas**, due to recent movements, publications, popular teachings, political strife, and societal stresses, the need for guidance concerning Christian citizenship and church-state relations has intensified; and **Whereas**, the _Westminster Standards_, as adopted by the Presbyterian Church in America and the Preface to the Presbyterian Church in America’s _Book of Church Order_ provide substantial, biblical guidance concerning Christian citizenship and the relationship of the church to the state; and **Whereas**, an explication of principles may prove more helpful than a focus on persons or rapidly changing movements, and may be helpful to the church in decades to come, **Therefore, be it resolved** that the 52 [nd] General Assembly of the Presbyterian Church in America direct its moderator to appoint an unfunded ad interim committee to produce a pastoral letter [45] or short paper applying the doctrine of the Constitution of the Presbyterian Church in America to questions of Christian citizenship and church-state relations for the edification of the Church. The committee will be composed of four teaching elders and three ruling elders and will set its own procedures and timelines. _Adopted by the Tennessee Valley Presbytery at its s

source: OVERTURE 49 from the Pacific Northwest Presbytery (to OC, AC, CC,
body: CDM, CTS, Geneva, MNA, MTW, PCAF, RH, RUF) “Erect ana d interim committee on AI” **Be it resolved** that the General Assembly erect a study committee of seven members, consisting of teaching or ruling elders, with an annual budget of $15,000 (funded by gifts to the Administrative Committee), tasked to: a. Write a report by the 54th General Assembly (June 2027) on the teaching of Scripture and the _Westminster Standards_, with a particular reference to our anthropology ( _WCF_ 4, 7) and ecclesiology ( _WCF_ 25), and suggest general principles for the use or limits of artificial intelligence technology (AI) in the Church; b. Then apply these general principles to the following suggested areas: i. _Pastoral Ministry_ : AI use in preaching, teaching, and pastoral care ii. _Church Administration_ : AI use in local churches, Presbyteries, and the General Assembly, also addressing security concerns iii. _Church Discipline_ : suggest criteria for assessing AIgenerated evidence in church courts, especially in disciplinary cases iv. _Christian Vocation and Diaconal Ministry_ : a Reformed approach to work and service amid AI-driven changes in the workplace and economy, with diaconal strategies for affected church members v. _Christian Education:_ implications for PCA institutions (e.g., Covenant College, Covenant Seminary), especially on education philosophy and practice vi. _Medical Ethics_ : boundaries for AI use in medical practice with particular emphasis in recovering natural function vs. enhancing human ability beyond natural limitations vii. _Evangelism and Apologetics_ : strat

source: OVERTURE 50 from the Chesapeake Presbytery (to OC)
body: “Encouragement to Discernment and Compassion regarding Immigrants” **Whereas**, Scripture commands believers to submit to and pray for governing authorities as instituted by God for the good of society and the restraint of evil (Romans 13:1–7; 1 Peter 2:13–17; 1 Timothy 2:1–2); and **Whereas**, the moral law of God, revealed in both Old and New Testaments, calls for the protection of human life and the care of the vulnerable— including the fatherless, the widow, the sojourner, and the oppressed (Exodus 22:21; Deuteronomy 10:18–19; Proverbs 31:8–9; Matthew 25:35–40); and **Whereas**, the _Westminster Larger Catechism_ teaches that the sixth commandment requires “all careful studies, and lawful endeavors, to preserve the life of ourselves and others” ( _WLC_ Q.135), and forbids “the neglecting or withdrawing the lawful and necessary means of preservation of life” ( _WLC_ Q.136); and **Whereas**, the Lord Jesus Himself was once a political refugee, having fled as an infant with His parents into Egypt to escape the violent policies of Herod (Matthew 2:13–15), and He taught that welcoming the stranger is integral to the life of discipleship (Matthew 25:35–36); and **Whereas**, many immigrants in the United States have entered due to political instability, persecution, or extreme poverty, and live peacefully and productively in our communities—often without access to legal status—yet remain vulnerable to exploitation, fear, and injustice; and **Whereas**, God's law commands compassion and justice for the sojourner: “You shall not wrong a sojourner or oppress him… You shall treat
